(Brouwer; Schrijvers), English (Ahl; Cobbold; Fagles; Fallon
and Fantham; Ferry; Lembke; Lombardo), Finnish (Lahdensuu
and Saastamoinen), German (Binder and Binder; Fink), Hebrew
(Dykman), Irish (Calder; Murray), Polish (Abramowiczówna;
Karylowski and Stabryla), Portuguese (Laender and Marques),
and Spanish (Bonifaz Ñuño; Espinosa Pólit, Ruiz, et al.).

title: Hesitant Heroes: Private Inhibition, Cultural Crisis. Ithaca and
reviews: P. Michelakis, CR 57 (2007) 133- 4. Hallett examines affinities between Sulpicia's poems and, among other topics, Vergil's treatment of Dido. Nadeau argues that Vergil establishes the harpy Celaeno as a trope for Sextus Pompeius. Solin shows that inscriptional testimony allows us to conclude that the river name Liris could also be used as a personal name (cf. Aen. 1 1.670). Ziolkowski explores the theme of heroic hesitation and its correlation with civic crisis through a discussion of the character of Aeneas and of a number of other heroes from antiquity to modernist times. title: Le Virgile d'Horace
publication_block: Promethe 84. Fréchet discusses Aen. 6.635-676. Knox suggests that the first book of Tibullus may have been published in 29 B.C. rather than in the mid-20s. This opens the door to a better contextualized examination of pastoral motifs in Tibullus and his relationship to Vergil's Eclogues. Lütkemeyer discusses Ovid's intertextual allusion to the first Eclogue. Pucci suggests that "The transgression Vergil and Horace practice in 1.3 is poetic composition itself." Putnam contains a chapter on Vergil

title: Eine Anspielung auf die Aeneas-Sage in HorazEpode 16?
publication_block: JAC 22: 131-9. Asztalos defends the transmitted reading "pluma" at Horace, carm. 4.10 on the ground that it alludes to Cycnus at Aen. 10.189-93 and, through this Vergilian filter, to carm. 2.20. Moretti observes Horace's possible allusion to Aen. 11.699-723. Other studies in this section have to do either with intertextuality or with shared themes

title: Shades of Virgil: Seneca's Troades
publication_block: MD 61: 191-210. Aiello notes allusions to Vergil and Ovid; Bernstein to Aen. 12.435-40; Bruggisser to Aen. 9.735-7. Ferenczi discusses Valerius Flaccus' use of the Aeneid. Freyburger-Galland shows that Dio Cassius 75.10.2 translates Aen. 11.371-3, and asserts that this passage is Dio's only reference to Vergil. Fucecchi (2007) points to Vergil's influence on Statius, Th. 12.105f., and (2008a) argues that Valerius Flaccus' Amazon at 5.614 is modeled on Camilla in the Aeneid. Gamberale demonstrates that Jerome, epist. 22 alludes to Aen. 3.173 and 10.642. Gärtner includes discussion of the influence of Vergil on Valerius Flaccus. Gioseffi argues that Martial 14.58, followed then by Juvenal 9.102 and Alcuin, carm. 32, alludes to Eel. 2.69; and discusses the reception of Geo. 4.489. Guillaumin notes citation of Aen. 7.234-8 by the gromaticus Siculus Flaccus. Horsfall demonstrates that in at least six instances the text of Lycophron's Alexandra is given point if considered as a variation upon Vergil's Latin. Murgatroyd shows that Valerius alludes to Ovid and to Aen. 8.193-7; and Passarella that Ambrosius, Par. 7.35 alludes to Geo. 4.485-93. Petringa remarks on the influence of Vergil on Cyprian's poetry. Pisano observes Vergilian influence on Apuleius' story of Cupid and Psyche. Santini discusses Aen. 7 as a model for Pun. 13.115-37. Squire notices that allusion to Vergil is made in an inscribed epigra Sperlonga (AE 1967.85)

Gamberale demonstrates that Jerome, epist. 22 alludes to Aen. 3.173 and 10.642. Gärtner includes discussion of the influence of Vergil on Valerius Flaccus. Gioseffi argues that Martial 14.58, followed then by Juvenal 9.102 and Alcuin, carm. 32, alludes to Eel. 2.69; and discusses the reception of Geo. 4.489. Guillaumin notes citation of Aen. 7.234-8 by the gromaticus Siculus Flaccus. Horsfall demonstrates that in at least six instances the text of Lycophron's Alexandra is given point if considered as a variation upon Vergil's Latin. Murgatroyd shows that Valerius alludes to Ovid and to Aen. 8.193-7; and Passarella that Ambrosius, Par. 7.35 alludes to Geo. 4.485-93. Petringa remarks on the influence of Vergil on Cyprian's poetry. Pisano observes Vergilian influence on Apuleius' story of Cupid and Psyche. Santini discusses Aen. 7 as a model for Pun. 13.115-37. Squire notices that allusion to Vergil is made in an inscribed epigra Sperlonga (AE 1967.85).

raw_text: Suerbaum, Werner. 2008. "Die Schildbeschreibung Vergils in Worten und Bildern zur Aeneis (8,608-731)." In Freund, Vielberg, et al. 2008:451-81. Steigerwald argues that the so-called Aphrodisius scene on the triumphal arch at S. Maria Maggiore in Rome portrays Augustus and Vergil rather than Aphrodisius and a Cynic philosopher. Suerbaum discusses paintings and book illustrations of Aeneas' shield that were created between 1 502 and 1 840.
raw_text_original:
Suerbaum, Werner. 2008. "Die Schildbeschreibung Vergils in
Worten und Bildern zur Aeneis (8,608-731)." In Freund, Viel-
berg, et al. 2008:451-81.
Steigerwald argues that the so-called Aphrodisius scene on the
triumphal arch at S. Maria Maggiore in Rome portrays Augustus
and Vergil rather than Aphrodisius and a Cynic philosopher.
Suerbaum discusses paintings and book illustrations of Aeneas'
shield that were created between 1 502 and 1 840.

Gärtner 2008 observes that Statius' epilogue alludes to Lucan 9.980-86 and thus imitates Vergil; Gärtner 2010 argues that the structure of Silius' Punica alludes in several ways to the structure of the Aeneid. Gasti is concerned with Faustus of Riez (ca. ad 405-ca. 490). Hulls shows that Ammianus5 unusual phrase "raising the standards of his obstinacy" at 14.1.10, describing the Caesar Gallus, is framed by a double allusion to Aen. 2.304-7 and 2.496-99 and must be a reference to a description of the emperor Domitian at Statius, Silv. 4.2. Magnelli discusses Calpurnius Siculus' main models, Theocritus and Vergil. Marshall demonstrates that Statius, Silv. 1.1 alludes to epic, and to the Aeneid in particular. Mazzucco touches on women's writing of Vergilian centones. Risi shows that Silius5 character Podaetus is a literary invention which owes much to Vergil's Achilles and to the Achilles of Statius' Achilleid. Van Mal-Maeder examines Endelechius' fourth-century ad poem De mortibus boum and discusses its debt to Vergil's Eclogues and Georgics , which are themselves indebted to Lucretius. Vieira discusses Lucan's dependence on Aen. 8.190-272. Chinnici discusses Calpurnius Siculus' allusions to Vergil. Gärtner 2008 observes that Statius' epilogue alludes to Lucan 9.980-86 and thus imitates Vergil; Gärtner 2010 argues that the structure of Silius' Punica alludes in several ways to the structure of the Aeneid. Gasti is concerned with Faustus of Riez (ca. ad 405-ca. 490). Hulls shows that Ammianus5 unusual phrase "raising the standards of his obstinacy" at 14.1.10, describing the Caesar Gallus, is framed by a double allusion to Aen. 2.304-7 and 2.496-99 and must be a reference to a description of the emperor Domitian at Statius, Silv. 4.2. Magnelli discusses Calpurnius Siculus' main models, Theocritus and Vergil. Marshall demonstrates that Statius, Silv. 1.1 alludes to epic, and to the Aeneid in particular. Mazzucco touches on women's writing of Vergilian centones. Risi shows that Silius5 character Podaetus is a literary invention which owes much to Vergil's Achilles and to the Achilles of Statius' Achilleid. Van Mal-Maeder examines Endelechius' fourth-century ad poem De mortibus boum and discusses its debt to Vergil's Eclogues and Georgics , which are themselves indebted to Lucretius. Vieira discusses Lucan's dependence on Aen. 8.190-272. of C. S. Lewis' translation of the Aeneid , published with the Latin text,

theorization of "the ways in which literary translation is a translation
into the literary" with a discussion of translations of the Aeneid.

Borghini offers the conjecture "et matan uitatas umbras" at Petronius
62.7 on the basis of Aen. 6.290-94. Chinnici discusses Calpurnius Siculus'
allusions to Vergil. Gärtner 2008 observes that Statius' epilogue alludes
to Lucan 9.980-86 and thus imitates Vergil; Gärtner 2010 argues that
the structure of Silius' Punica alludes in several ways to the structure of
the Aeneid. Gasti is concerned with Faustus of Riez (ca. ad 405-ca. 490).
Hulls shows that Ammianus5 unusual phrase "raising the standards of
his obstinacy" at 14.1.10, describing the Caesar Gallus, is framed by a
double allusion to Aen. 2.304-7 and 2.496-99 and must be a reference
to a description of the emperor Domitian at Statius, Silv. 4.2. Magnelli
discusses Calpurnius Siculus' main models, Theocritus and Vergil.
Marshall demonstrates that Statius, Silv. 1.1 alludes to epic, and to the
Aeneid in particular. Mazzucco touches on women's writing of Vergilian
centones. Risi shows that Silius5 character Podaetus is a literary invention
which owes much to Vergil's Achilles and to the Achilles of Statius'
Achilleid. Van Mal-Maeder examines Endelechius' fourth-century ad poem
De mortibus boum and discusses its debt to Vergil's Eclogues and Georgics ,
which are themselves indebted to Lucretius. Vieira discusses Lucan's
dependence on Aen. 8.190-272. Wulff Alonso studies the reception of
Homer, especially of the Iliad, in ancient India; in an appendix, he explores
the possible relationship between the Mababharata and the Aeneid.

Itsumi observes that, since ancient didactic poetry was considered to
be part of epic, the didactic poet was usually inspired by a rhetorical
rather than generic model. Vergil and Ovid seem to be exceptions in their
awareness of the genre they helped to create.

Fedeli discusses the implications of Propertius' echo, at 4.1.1-4, of Aen. 8.188, where Evander addresses Aeneas as "hospes Troiane." Gordon writes that Vergil and other Augustan poets play with the theme of magic especially in relation to the world of courtesans and brothels. Cavarzere notes that Horace's "sublimi feriam sidera" echoes "sublime ferent
ad sidera" in Eel. 9.29. Egelhaaf-Gaiser writes on Ovid, Her. 1. Fedeli discusses
the implications of Propertius' echo, at 4.1.1-4, of Aen. 8.188, where Evander
addresses Aeneas as "hospes Troiane." Gordon writes that Vergil and other
Augustan poets play with the theme of magic especially in relation to the world
of courtesans and brothels. At the same time, through a poetic transformation
of this discourse, the poets manage to distance themselves from that world.
That should not prevent us from looking at these texts once again within the
larger social discourse about magic. Citations from and allusions to Eel. 8 in the
Pompeian graffiti should be contemplated within this context.

Prominent in the ancient criticism of Vergil, writes Powell, was the
notion that Aeneas narration in Book 2 has the nature of a rhetorical self-
defense. An analysis of his narrative reveals features that have the rhetorical
functions of exonerating Aeneas from blame and of presenting his actions
in the best possible light. An appreciation of this rhetoric can enhance our
appreciation of the subtlety of Vergils character presentation.

One of the inscriptions discussed by Escolà Tuset- from the Christian
necropolis of Torrebaja in Pueblonuevo del Guadiana (Badajoz) and
dating from the last third of the fourth century or first half of the fifth- is
an adaptation and imitation of a verse from Vergil. Gómez Pallarès writes
that the relationship between epigraphic texts and Vergil has always been
studied on the assumption that Vergils work (mainly the Aeneid) influenced
the inscriptions. But epigraphic literature written before Vergil influenced
his poetry in turn. Gómez Pallarès notes that the end of the Aeneid contains
specialized epigraphic vocabulary and argues that Turnus death is presented
as a mors immatura. Grandazzi s reexamination of unpublished notes from
the excavations of 1956-82 on the site of Augustus residence, as well as
evidence from new excavations on the Palatine, show that Aeneid 7.170-94
realistically describes the building where the Senate met. Hui compares the
leading of the defeated Carthaginians through Rome, in book 8 of Petrarchs
Africa ; Aeneas tour of Arcadia, in book 8 of the Aeneid; and Caesars
visit to Troy, in Book 9 of Lucans Pharsalia , in order to explore what they
tell us about how time past and time present can coexist within a single
topographic narrative. Kondratieff sees Anchises, like Aeneas, as a pattern
of Augustus, as Anchises survey of his progeny reflects Augustus censorial
activity: Augustus conducted his first census (without holding the office of
censor) in 28 BC.

title: Augustine's Mixed Feelings: Vergil's Aeneid and the Psalms of David in the Confessions
publication_block: HThR 102 (2009) 453-79. Okácová, Marie. "Mythological Epyllia Written in the Form of Virgilian Centos: A Model Case of Intertextuality." SPFB(klas) 15 (2010) 139-54. Willis, I. K. A. Now and Rome: Lucan and Vergil as Theorists of Politics and Space. Continuum Studies in Classical Reception. London: Continuum, 201 1. Reviewed: A. Augoustakis, JRS 102 (2012) 389-90. Coffee et al. describe a digital approach to intertextual study involving the creation of a free online tool for the automatic detection of parallel phrases. A test comparison of the Aeneid and Lucans Bellum civile shows that the tool can identify meaningful intertexts. Fratantuono notices that the battle narrative at Valerius Flaccus 6.515-41 echoes Aeneid 11. Gellérfi writes that the walk around Rome in Aeneid 8 is not the only model for Caesar s (unhistoric) visit to the ruined Troy in Lucan, but also the katabasis in Aeneid 6. Hulls writes that both Statius and Silius Italicus shaped 124 - Shirley Werner Vergilian tradition in idiosyncratic ways, using metaphorical methods of looking back at Vergilian poetry. The basis for their reductive readings of Vergil can be found in the wider reading systems of the early imperial period, in particular those that involve Vergil as an educational text and those that involve systematized parody of Vergil. Maciver discusses the famous intertextual nexus evoked by a line in Quintus Smyrnaeus: at Quintus Smyrnaeus 14, Helen assuages Menelaus by stating that she left his home and bed unwillingly. Her words echo those of Aeneas at Aeneid 6.460; those of Medea at Apollonius Rhodius 4.1021-22; those of the coma Berenices at Catullus 66.39; and those of the coma Berenices in Callimachus, fr. 110 Pfeiffer. McCarthy reminds us that Vergils Aeneid and the Psalter attributed to David so influenced Augustines writing that the Confessions have been called a recapitulation of Vergilian epic in a Christian universe and also have been described as an amplified Psalter. Classicists and theologians have studied the individual influence of these works in Augustine s Confessions , but never in relation to each other. Augustine presents the Aeneid and the Psalms as embodying incompatible forms of life. For Augustine, Vergils epic produces a template for an imperial culture that institutionalizes a disordered drive to exalt oneself over others and to effect ones own will. The Psalms, by contrast, offer a therapeutic program to heal a disordered fixation on self and to help a person cling to God. Power 2012 discusses the similarity of an anecdote on Tiberius' treatment of a jester- reported by both Suetonius, Tib. 57.2, and Dio 57.14.1-2- to Priams slaughter in Aeneid 2.535-50. A unique variation in Suetonius (recipere debitum ) contains a verbal and contextual similarity to Vergils passage ( praemia ... debita , Aen . 2.537-38). Power 2010 argues that Pliny, epist. 5.10, alludes to Suetonius life of Vergil and that these allusions are part of a wider network of allusions, both within this particular letter and more generally in Pliny s Book 5, that revolve around the theme of unpublished writings. Verino claims to be following in the footsteps of Homer, Vergil, and Dante. Strobl offers observations concerning the Aeneid commentary by Domizio Calderini (1444/46-1478). For pragmatic reasons, I do not often cite in this bibliography works that do not focus on Vergil as a major topic. This is especially true in Dante studies, where it is almost impossible to avoid mentioning Vergil. Thus I have not previously cited Wetherbees 2009 book. Galligans 2013 review (185) has persuaded me that it ought to be included for its insightful reading not only of Vergil but of Lucan, of Lucan s reading of Vergil, and of Dantes reading of both. She writes: "Virgil is the first to be examined, and he remains sharply in focus throughout the book, as the discussion of each of the other poets [Statius and Lucan] rests to a large extent on their readings of Virgil. ... Wetherbee suggests that, as an act of poetry, Lucan read Virgil much as Dante does, but that Lucan offers Dante what Virgil cannot: anger, horror, and the grotesque. raw_text: Pontani examines the relationship of Aeneid 8.18-41 to Apollonius Rhodes 3.756-60. Romani Mistretta notes that the phrase ingenti percussu amore in Georg. 2.476 alludes to Lucretius 1.922-25, mediated by Horac Epod. 11.1-2. Scafoglio 201 1 discusses the reception of Stesichorus in Vergil Scafoglio 2012 discusses a notorious inconsistency. At Aeneid 2.554-58, Vergil seems to follow a version of the death of Priam that differs from th 126 - Shirley Werner murder he has described at 2.547-53: Priams headless corpse is abandoned on the Trojan shore as if he was murdered there. It can be argued that Vergils possible allusion to the death of Pompey holds only a secondary, marginal meaning in the episode, which should be evaluated independently from historic allegory. Servius, Aeneid 2.557, invokes an unspecified tragedy by Pacuvius. In light of Vergils imitation, Servius comments on Aeneid 2.506 and 557 and Pacuvius, fr. 294 Schieri, can be connected with Pacuvius Hermionay and in particular to the messenger's speech about Neoptolemus death, which is the divine vengeance invoked by Vergils Priam. Fressura 2009 discusses two bilingual Vergilian glosses. Huyck suggests
placing a full stop after maesti and retaining mouebant at Aeneid 9.471;
the implied subject is now the Rutulians. At 9.481-82, punctuate tune ille ,
senectae sera meae requies? and at 9.486 read, instead of nec te tua fuñera
mater produxi , P. Hofman Peerlkamps conjecture, nec fletu fuñera mater
produxi . Kovacs and Omrani observe that the phrase molli paulatim
flauescet campus arista at Ecl 4.28 is difficult because, although the next two
lines describe miraculous occurrences, the yellowing of the grain in the field
is not at all miraculous. Since we need a word indicating a place unsuitable
for farming, the word to be queried is campus . We might consider saltus.

the philological and exegetical studies of several authors, and in particular
of Vergil. Scappaticcio 2012b provides an edition and commentary on a

54 and 1.402-17 in this Vergilian manuscript is sometimes dependent on
Servius but in other places unique.

Buckley argues that Marlowes play responds to an Ovidian tradi
in which Aeneas betrays not only Dido, but also Troy. Respondin
Lydgates medieval Troy Book , Marlowe inserts an imposter Aeneas in
play. Lovatt discusses the poem Carliasy by the Florentine Ugolino Ver
Vergilian Bibliography - 129
(1438-1516), a fifteen-book epic written in Latin hexameters about the
exploits of Charlemagne. Verino claims to be following in the footsteps of
Homer, Vergil, and Dante. Strobl offers observations concerning the Aeneid
commentary by Domizio Calderini (1444/46-1478).
For pragmatic reasons, I do not often cite in this bibliography works
that do not focus on Vergil as a major topic. This is especially true in Dante
studies, where it is almost impossible to avoid mentioning Vergil. Thus I
have not previously cited Wetherbees 2009 book. Galligans 2013 review
(185) has persuaded me that it ought to be included for its insightful reading
not only of Vergil but of Lucan, of Lucan s reading of Vergil, and of Dantes
reading of both. She writes: "Virgil is the first to be examined, and he
remains sharply in focus throughout the book, as the discussion of each of
the other poets [Statius and Lucan] rests to a large extent on their readings
of Virgil. ... Wetherbee suggests that, as an act of poetry, Lucan read Virgil
much as Dante does, but that Lucan offers Dante what Virgil cannot: anger,
horror, and the grotesque. He provides a nuanced reading of Dantes reading
of Lucan, arguing for Dantes awareness of both the Roman poets irony, and
his mockery of epic machinery."

McDermott writes about the influence of Hesiod and of Vergils Georgias
on Charles Frazier s novel Cold Mountain . Pogorzelski traces a systematic
correspondence between the "Circe" episode of James Joyces Ulysses and
the sixth book of the Aeneid. Schiesaro suggests that Richard Heinzes
familiarity with eighteenth-century authors played a fundamental role
in his innovative reading of the Aeneid. Tatum contemplates the ways in
which historical and political circumstances shaped the responses of many
twentieth-century scholars (Viktor Pöschl, Gerda Seligson, Simone Weil,
Michael Putnam, and others) to the Aeneid.

Guastella writes about the way in which Vergils personification of Fama
was transformed into the concept of Gloria , as attested by Macrobius and
Boethius. The thirteenth- and fourteenth-century manuscripts of two work
by Petrarch provide evidence for the iconography associated with thi
concept.

371 SB), including an analysis of Vergilian influence. Iulietto writes that it is
possible to posit a number of correspondences between the representation
of Cleopatra in De bello Actiaco and Vergils Dido. Kersten observes that
Lucan 2.596-609 alludes to Georg. 3.224-41 and Aen. 12.101-6 and 716-
22, passages that question Pompeys suitability as a traditional epic hero.
The previously unnoticed acrostic "ipse nequit" in these lines supports
this interpretation. Malamud shows how Hosidius Getas tragedy Medea ,
a Vergilian cento, exhibits the allusivity, word play, and interest in formal
structures that characterize Latin literature from Africa. Montemayor
Aceves offers a Latin text and annotated Spanish translation of Suetoniuss
Life of Vergil. Papaioannou discusses the relationship between politics and
the bucolic world in Vergils Eclogues and Aeneid and the relevance of this
relationship to Lucans poem. Paschalis sets Petronius, Sat. 79-99, against the
backdrop of the Aeneid. Pomer Monferrer observes that Christian authors,
in particular Tertullian, Jerome, and Augustine, regarded Dido as a model
of monogamy, in contrast to Vergils emphasis on her role as Aeneas s lover.
Wolff observes that when Flavian poets drew analogies between the reigns
of Domitian and Augustus, the Domitianic era was found to be inferior in
that poets did not have patrons like Maecenas. For Martial, Vergils glory
could be identified with the glory of Augustus's reign.

Harrill considers the influence of female readers of the Aeneid on two
medieval adaptations, the eleventh- century Encomium Emmae reginae
and the twelfth-century Roman ďEnéas. The latter is tailored to a female
audience, while the former is tailored for a female patron. Shumilin
demonstrates that the accessus to the Aeneid by Zono de' Magnalis (early
fourteenth century) presents an unusual variant of a Greco-Roman theory
concerning the relationship between fact and fiction in poetic texts. Zono
viewed historia as an objective, but not necessarily true, account; rather, it
is an account that exists outside of (and before) the texts produced from it.
"Historia is imagined as the material that, say, Virgil had before him, prior to
164 - Shirley Werner
the start of his work on the Aeneid." Smolak examines two twelfth-century
allegorical commentaries on Martianus Capellas De nuptiis Mercurii et
Philologiae and the first six books of Vergil's Aeneid , written for use in the
cathedral schools, in order to demonstrate the unity of philosophy, which
comprises both knowledge of the universe and ethics. It is not improbable
that Bernardus Silvestris was the author of both commentaries.

Barnard explains (131): "John Dryden's translation of The Works of Virgil [was] the first example of a major literary work by a living English writer published by subscription. … Imposed as a folio in fours, the original publication plans called for two classes of subscribers. The first subscribers, who numbered 101 and paid five guineas apiece in two installments, were to have a fine-paper copy with a special inserted plate dedicated to each of them. … The second subscribers, whose number was unlimited, were to pay two guineas, also in installments, and receive a small-paper copy. In addition to £200 in copy money paid in stages, Dryden was to benefit from a portion of the subscription fees and any gifts from patrons. The translation was thus poised between a publication based on patronage and a commercial venture." Barnard contextualizes the publication by analyzing its printing history in light of the political struggle between Dryden and his publisher. Cobbold gives a prose translation of the Aeneid with illustrations and appendices. Corsalini writes about the translation of Aeneid 2 into Italian by Giacomo Leopardi (1798-1837). Barnard explains (131): "John Dryden's translation of The Works of Virgil [was] the first example of a major literary work by a living English writer published by subscription. … Imposed as a folio in fours, the original publication plans called for two classes of subscribers. The first subscribers, who numbered 101 and paid five guineas apiece in two installments, were to have a fine-paper copy with a special inserted plate dedicated to each of them. … The second subscribers, whose number was unlimited, were to pay two guineas, also in installments, and receive a small-paper copy. In addition to £200 in copy money paid in stages, Dryden was to benefit from a portion of the subscription fees and any gifts from patrons. The translation was thus poised between a publication based on patronage and a commercial venture." Barnard contextualizes the publication by analyzing its printing history in light of the political struggle between Dryden and his publisher. Barnard explains (131): "John Dryden's translation of The Works of
Virgil [was] the first example of a major literary work by a living English
writer published by subscription. … Imposed as a folio in fours, the original
publication plans called for two classes of subscribers. The first subscribers,
who numbered 101 and paid five guineas apiece in two installments, were
to have a fine-paper copy with a special inserted plate dedicated to each
of them. … The second subscribers, whose number was unlimited, were
to pay two guineas, also in installments, and receive a small-paper copy.
In addition to £200 in copy money paid in stages, Dryden was to benefit
from a portion of the subscription fees and any gifts from patrons. The
translation was thus poised between a publication based on patronage and
a commercial venture." Barnard contextualizes the publication by analyzing
its printing history in light of the political struggle between Dryden and his
publisher. Cobbold gives a prose translation of the Aeneid with illustrations
and appendices. Corsalini writes about the translation of Aeneid 2 into
Italian by Giacomo Leopardi (1798-1837). Fo considers the difficulties
facing a translator of the Aeneid and includes, in appendix (359-62), a
"Tabella sulle traduzioni virgiliane (1500-1979)" by Ida Paoloni. García
Jurado writes about the ideas of Jorge Luis Borges (1899-1986) concerning
the protean character of literature and the phenomenon of translation.

Gagliardi argues in "Propemptikòn" that Ecl. 10.46-49 should be
understood as witness to Gallus's final poetic words. Gardner discusses the
cattle plague in Georg. 3 as a metaphor for civil discord and compares it to
Ovid's account of plague in Met. 7.490-660. Hardie observes that the figure
of Scylla in Ovid's Metamorphoses derives especially from Ecl. 1.404-9 and
Aen. 3.424-28. Jaeger observes that Horace's Satires make little reference
to cheese and milk because they are symbols of the pastoral world, while
"Vergil's Georgics and Eclogues claim cheese for the country, yet make it a
link via commerce to the city. Varro's De re rustica places cheese on the
margins: its proper place now is away from Rome; and the right time for
it at Rome was before the city's foundation" (63). Stachon observes that
Propertius 1.8a, where the poet imagines Cynthia in the frozen north, not
only uses a motif from Gallus to which Vergil alludes in Ecl. 10.46-49 but
also gestures explicitly, and polemically, towards Vergil in using the unusual
term Vergiliae for the Pleiades. Tronchet argues that Ovid's characterization
of Anna at Fasti 3.545-656 is shaped not only by the intertextual influence

Cairns observes that a detail from Vergil's instructions for making a
plough at Georg. 1.173-74 may be understood in light of his interest in
learned pseudo-etymologies: in his use of the word fagus (beech) he is
aware of Greek φηγȩȢ (oak), and the wood recommended here may well
be the oak. Vergil may have drawn on scholia to Hesiod. Dinter takes the
theoretical approach of intermediality to provide a framework for exploring
the blurring of inscriptional and narrative modes in Latin poetry. He focuses
on instances in the Aeneid of the expression tu/te quoque (translated from

the Greek epigraphic formula καὶ σὺ), which bears sepulchral connotations
and foreshadows death. Minchin compares Homer's approach to storytelling
in the Iliad with Vergil's in the Aeneid, observing that the latter was a written
poem presented first to a listening audience and subsequently to an audience
of readers. Molero Alcaraz studies the uses of the noun fama in the works of
Vergil when it is used in the sense of what is said collectively about someone
or something. Short defines the word sermo in imagistic terms in order to
explain its polysemy between monologic and dialogic interpretations and
also its association with Mercury, arguing, with evidence from the Aeneid,
that the symbolic sameness of sermo and Mercury underlies a systematic
slippage of meaning between these signs. Weber writes that the sense in
which recognoscere is used in Aen. 8.721 is unparalleled in verse but common
in prose as a technical term belonging to the specialized vocabulary of
government and law. A similar analysis may be applied to recensere, used
synonymously with recognoscere at 6.682.

De Gianni observes that Ambrose alludes to "dolum ridens" in Ecl. 6.24. Emberger considers Vergil as a model for Calpurnius Siculus. Galli, "Insidie palesi," considers Hosidius Geta's Medea and the Vergiliocentones minores in the Codex Salmasianus (Paris, Bibliothèque nationale, Lat. 10318). Jakobi writes about the influence of Vergil on the sixth-century epic poet Corippus. Kenens examines the text and reception of the Iudicium coci et pistoris iudice Vulcano (AL 190 Shackleton Bailey), probably composed in the fourth century, which makes parodic reference to Vergilian epic. Klaassen observes that Silius's narrative technique in his Punica was drawn from Vergil: Hannibal appears an antitype of Aeneas, while Scipio is the Roman Aeneas; other characters also assume the role of Aeneas in different ways. McGill discusses the prose passage inserted into the De ecclesia in the Codex Salmasianus and the name "Maro Iunior" claimed by the writer of the cento. O'Hogan, "Intertextual Journey," comments on the many echoes of the Aeneid, notably of passages in which Juno plays a role, in Prudentius, who presents himself, as narrator, as a double of Aeneas. Paolucci, "Per la costituzione del testo dell'Alcesta," continues her discussion of the text preserved in the Codex Salmasianus from line 145. Paolucci, "Rileggendo," discusses a number of difficult passages in the cento Versus ad gratiam Domini, basing her observations on C. Arcidiacono's 2011 edition. Trinacty examines the repetition, by Lucan and Silius Italicus, of a bilingual pun found in the Aeneid. In the epilogue to his edition of selected papers on poetry and history, Woodman observes that the narratives of Latin historians exhibit a fundamentally poetic element. He takes as a methodological case study the relationship between Tacitus, Ann. 15.38-40, on the great fire in Rome under Nero, and Aen. 2, on the destruction of Troy. Barchiesi, Alessandro. Stok, "Triumuiri agris diuidendis," writes about the expropriation of land mentioned by Vergil in the Eclogues in the context of Servius's claim that a commission of three (triumviri agris dividendis)-composed of Alfenus Varus, Asinius Pollio, and Cornelius Gallus-managed these expropriations. Zanker argues that assumptions we have about expressions of meaning may mislead us into adopting an interpretation of a phrase common in Servius-"hoc vult dicere"-as if it were equivalent to "ça veut dire." It is more likely, he writes, that Vergil is the subject of vult and that hoc serves either as a direct object or as an ablative of means. Burnyeat responds to the modern critical assumption that Táin Bó Cúailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley) is the medieval Irish equivalent to the Aeneid, and to the late-antique and medieval commonplace that, in his relationship to his Homeric sources, Vergil was as much a compilator as he was an auctor. Carlson writes about the literary rivalry between John Gower (ca. 1330- 1408) and Geoffrey Chaucer (ca. 1340-1400), observing that "Gower's rivalry with Chaucer was only part of his manifold literary antagonism, however, encompassing not only a senior poet's rivalry with a junior, but also Gower's rivalry with an ancient author, whom Chaucer too used but Gower knew better, Ovid" (932). In his conception of "vaga Fama" at Visio Anglie 1231-32, however, Gower refers to Aen. 4.173-96, "when generally otherwise he is Ovidian, in reply to Chaucer's uncharacteristic usage, preferring Ovid [when he constructs his image of Fama in House of Fame, 713-20] where more often he used Virgil" (951). Christ discusses Heinrich von Veldeke's Eneit (ca. 1175), written in a Dutch literary dialect; its model, the anonymous Old French Roman d'Énéas (ca. 1160); and Vergil's Aeneid. Cornelius situates the moral and political thought of John Gower's Visio Anglie (1381) within its Vergilian intertext. Deist compares Vergil's imagery with that of the Roman d'Énéas. Eitel writes about the influence of the Eclogues on Dante and on his contemporary Giovanni del Virgilio (d. 1319). Mordeglia analyzes a tenth-century allegorical poem that alludes to a number of classical authors through a late-antique and medieval poetic filter; the allusions include forty-four citations of Vergil, with a directness of citation that suggests a parodic treatment. Nazzaro writes about the development of the Christian interpretation of Vergil, especially of the fourth eclogue, in the patristic and medieval worlds, with attention to Lactantius, Constantine, Augustine, Quodvultdeus, and Dante. De Gianni observes that Ambrose alludes to "dolum ridens" in Ecl. 6.24. Emberger considers Vergil as a model for Calpurnius Siculus. Galli, "Insidie palesi," considers Hosidius Geta's Medea and the Vergiliocentones minores in the Codex Salmasianus (Paris, Bibliothèque nationale, Lat. 10318). Jakobi writes about the influence of Vergil on the sixth-century epic poet Corippus. Kenens examines the text and reception of the Iudicium coci et pistoris iudice Vulcano (AL 190 Shackleton Bailey), probably composed in the fourth century, which makes parodic reference to Vergilian epic. Klaassen observes that Silius's narrative technique in his Punica was drawn from Vergil: Hannibal appears an antitype of Aeneas, while Scipio is the Roman Aeneas; other characters also assume the role of Aeneas in different ways. McGill discusses the prose passage inserted into the De ecclesia in the Codex Salmasianus and the name "Maro Iunior" claimed by the writer of the cento. O'Hogan, "Intertextual Journey," comments on the many echoes of the Aeneid, notably of passages in which Juno plays a role, in Prudentius, who presents himself, as narrator, as a double of Aeneas. Paolucci, "Per la costituzione del testo dell'Alcesta," continues her discussion of the text preserved in the Codex Salmasianus from line 145. Paolucci, "Rileggendo," discusses a number of difficult passages in the cento Versus ad gratiam Domini, basing her observations on C. Arcidiacono's 2011 edition. Trinacty examines the repetition, by Lucan and Silius Italicus, of a bilingual pun found in the Aeneid. In the epilogue to his edition of selected papers on poetry and history, Woodman observes that the narratives of Latin historians exhibit a fundamentally poetic element. He takes as a methodological case study the relationship between Tacitus, Ann. 15.38-40, on the great fire in Rome under Nero, and Aen. 2, on the destruction of Troy. Barchiesi, Alessandro. Delvigo, "'Mythici' vs 'physici'," examines the relationship of physics and cosmology to myth in Servius's commentary on the Aeneid, drawing comparative evidence from the writings of Augustine. Delvigo, "Servio e l'analogia," discusses Servius, Georg. 4.219-27. Gioseffi, "Come nasce un commento?," starts with a passage in Seneca (Dial. 10.9.2-4) as background for the analysis of the formula id est in Latin prose and in late-antique commentaries to Vergil, paying particular attention to the work of Tiberius Claudius Donatus and to the different types of notes introduced with this phrase. Lucarini offers improvements to the text of a previously missing section of the sixth book of Donatus's Interpretationes Vergilianae as edited by P. K. Marshall, Manuscripta 37 (1993) 3-20. The text is preserved in Vatican City, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, Vat. lat. 8222, fols. 2r-9v. Monno observes that Servius's remarks at Aen. 1.381 and 3.522 on the use of conscendere and the meaning of humilis imply elements of a physical theory. Pirovano, "Glosse, 2," studies the interlinear and marginal glosses in Bern, Burgerbibliothek 165. Ramires comments on Jeunet-Mancy's Commentaire sur l'Énéide de Virgile: Livre VI (Paris: Les Belles Lettres, 2012). Squillante Saccone discusses passages from Donatus. Stok's "Esegesi antiche" focuses on Servius. Stok, "Triumuiri agris diuidendis," writes about the expropriation of land mentioned by Vergil in the Eclogues in the context of Servius's claim that a commission of three (triumviri agris dividendis)-composed of Alfenus Varus, Asinius Pollio, and Cornelius Gallus-managed these expropriations. Zanker argues that assumptions we have about expressions of meaning may mislead us into adopting an interpretation of a phrase common in Servius-"hoc vult dicere"-as if it were equivalent to "ça veut dire." It is more likely, he writes, that Vergil is the subject of vult and that hoc serves either as a direct object or as an ablative of means. Woodbridge, Suffolk, UK: Boydell and Brewer, 2014. Bretzigheimer's topic is the twelfth-century Ylias, which takes as its models the Ilias Latina, Ovid's Metamorphoses, and the Aeneid. Burnyeat responds to the modern critical assumption that Táin Bó Cúailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley) is the medieval Irish equivalent to the Aeneid, and to the late-antique and medieval commonplace that, in his relationship to his Homeric sources, Vergil was as much a compilator as he was an auctor. Carlson writes about the literary rivalry between John Gower (ca. 1330- 1408) and Geoffrey Chaucer (ca. 1340-1400), observing that "Gower's rivalry with Chaucer was only part of his manifold literary antagonism, however, encompassing not only a senior poet's rivalry with a junior, but also Gower's rivalry with an ancient author, whom Chaucer too used but Gower knew better, Ovid" (932). In his conception of "vaga Fama" at Visio Anglie 1231-32, however, Gower refers to Aen. 4.173-96, "when generally otherwise he is Ovidian, in reply to Chaucer's uncharacteristic usage, preferring Ovid [when he constructs his image of Fama in House of Fame, 713-20] where more often he used Virgil" (951). Christ discusses Heinrich von Veldeke's Eneit (ca. 1175), written in a Dutch literary dialect; its model, the anonymous Old French Roman d'Énéas (ca. 1160); and Vergil's Aeneid. Cornelius situates the moral and political thought of John Gower's Visio Anglie (1381) within its Vergilian intertext. Deist compares Vergil's imagery with that of the Roman d'Énéas. Eitel writes about the influence of the Eclogues on Dante and on his contemporary Giovanni del Virgilio (d. 1319). Mordeglia analyzes a tenth-century allegorical poem that alludes to a number of classical authors through a late-antique and medieval poetic filter; the allusions include forty-four citations of Vergil, with a directness of citation that suggests a parodic treatment. Nazzaro writes about the development of the Christian interpretation of Vergil, especially of the fourth eclogue, in the patristic and medieval worlds, with attention to Lactantius, Constantine, Augustine, Quodvultdeus, and Dante. Wingfield's book contains a chapter 178 - Shirley Werner on "Gavin Douglas' Eneados" (150-77). The Scottish poet Gavin Douglas (ca. 1476-1522) produced the first complete translation of the Aeneid into English (1513). Alarcos Martínez, Miguel. examines the repetition, by Lucan and Silius Italicus, of a bilingual pun found

history, Woodman observes that the narratives of Latin historians exhibit
a fundamentally poetic element. He takes as a methodological case study
the relationship between Tacitus, Ann. 15.38-40, on the great fire in Rome

Clément-Tarantino discusses passages in Servius. Delvigo, "'Mythici'
vs 'physici'," examines the relationship of physics and cosmology to myth
in Servius's commentary on the Aeneid, drawing comparative evidence
from the writings of Augustine. Delvigo, "Servio e l'analogia," discusses
Servius, Georg. 4.219-27. Gioseffi, "Come nasce un commento?," starts
with a passage in Seneca (Dial. 10.9.2-4) as background for the analysis
of the formula id est in Latin prose and in late-antique commentaries to
Vergil, paying particular attention to the work of Tiberius Claudius Donatus
and to the different types of notes introduced with this phrase. Lucarini
offers improvements to the text of a previously missing section of the sixth
book of Donatus's Interpretationes Vergilianae as edited by P. K. Marshall,
Manuscripta 37 (1993) 3-20. The text is preserved in Vatican City, Biblioteca
Apostolica Vaticana, Vat. lat. 8222, fols. 2r-9v. Monno observes that Servius's
remarks at Aen. 1.381 and 3.522 on the use of conscendere and the meaning
of humilis imply elements of a physical theory. Pirovano, "Glosse, 2," studies
the interlinear and marginal glosses in Bern, Burgerbibliothek 165. Ramires
comments on Jeunet-Mancy's Commentaire sur l'Énéide de Virgile: Livre
VI (Paris: Les Belles Lettres, 2012). Squillante Saccone discusses passages
from Donatus. Stok's "Esegesi antiche" focuses on Servius. Stok, "Triumuiri
agris diuidendis," writes about the expropriation of land mentioned by
Vergil in the Eclogues in the context of Servius's claim that a commission
of three (triumviri agris dividendis)-composed of Alfenus Varus, Asinius
Pollio, and Cornelius Gallus-managed these expropriations. Zanker argues
that assumptions we have about expressions of meaning may mislead us
into adopting an interpretation of a phrase common in Servius-"hoc vult
dicere"-as if it were equivalent to "ça veut dire." It is more likely, he writes,
that Vergil is the subject of vult and that hoc serves either as a direct object
or as an ablative of means.

Bretzigheimer's topic is the twelfth-century Ylias, which takes as its
models the Ilias Latina, Ovid's Metamorphoses, and the Aeneid. Burnyeat
responds to the modern critical assumption that Táin Bó Cúailnge (The
Cattle Raid of Cooley) is the medieval Irish equivalent to the Aeneid, and
to the late-antique and medieval commonplace that, in his relationship to
his Homeric sources, Vergil was as much a compilator as he was an auctor.
Carlson writes about the literary rivalry between John Gower (ca. 1330-
1408) and Geoffrey Chaucer (ca. 1340-1400), observing that "Gower's
rivalry with Chaucer was only part of his manifold literary antagonism,
however, encompassing not only a senior poet's rivalry with a junior, but
also Gower's rivalry with an ancient author, whom Chaucer too used but
Gower knew better, Ovid" (932). In his conception of "vaga Fama" at Visio
Anglie 1231-32, however, Gower refers to Aen. 4.173-96, "when generally
otherwise he is Ovidian, in reply to Chaucer's uncharacteristic usage,
preferring Ovid [when he constructs his image of Fama in House of Fame,
713-20] where more often he used Virgil" (951). Christ discusses Heinrich
von Veldeke's Eneit (ca. 1175), written in a Dutch literary dialect; its model,
the anonymous Old French Roman d'Énéas (ca. 1160); and Vergil's Aeneid.
Cornelius situates the moral and political thought of John Gower's Visio
Anglie (1381) within its Vergilian intertext. Deist compares Vergil's imagery
with that of the Roman d'Énéas. Eitel writes about the influence of the
Eclogues on Dante and on his contemporary Giovanni del Virgilio (d. 1319).
Mordeglia analyzes a tenth-century allegorical poem that alludes to a number
of classical authors through a late-antique and medieval poetic filter; the
allusions include forty-four citations of Vergil, with a directness of citation
that suggests a parodic treatment. Nazzaro writes about the development of
the Christian interpretation of Vergil, especially of the fourth eclogue, in the
patristic and medieval worlds, with attention to Lactantius, Constantine,
Augustine, Quodvultdeus, and Dante. Wingfield's book contains a chapter

on "Gavin Douglas' Eneados" (150-77). The Scottish poet Gavin Douglas

Leuven: Leuven University Press, 2013. Putnam, Michael C. J. "Virgil and Sannazaro's Ekphrastic Vision." Ramus 40 (2011): 73-86. Raschieri, Amedeo Alessandro. "Edizioni virgiliane nel Cinquecento piemontese." Bollettino storico-bibliografico subalpino 113 (2015): 137-46. Rogerson, Anne. "Vegio's Ascanius: Problems in the Continuation of the Aeneid." CRJ 5 (2013): 106-25. Edgecombe's notes include discussion of an allusion to the Aeneid in a play by the English writer John Lyly (ca. 1553 or 1554-1606). Putnam writes about the influence of Vergil on the ekphrastic technique of Neapolitan humanist Jacopo Sannazaro (1458-1530) in his De partu virginis. Rogerson reinterprets Maffeo Vegio's fifteenth-century Supplement to the "Aeneid," observing that "Vegio does not, as some contemporaries did, criticize Aeneas overtly. He does, however, pointedly sideline and diminish the figure of Aeneas' son, Ascanius, to the extent that his succession to his father's position becomes doubtful" (106). Paradisi, Patrizia. "Nell' 'anno santo della patria' 1911: La presenza di Virgilio negli inni bilingui A Roma e A Torino di Giovanni Pascoli." AVM 79-80 (2011-12): 363-400. Torlone, Zara Martirosova. "Russian Tityrus: Joseph Brodsky in Arcadia." CRJ 5 (2013): 285-98. Paradisi studies Vergil's influence on the Latin hexameter hymns written by the poet Giovanni Pascoli (1855-1912) to celebrate the fiftieth anniversary of Italy's unification in 1911. Torlone analyzes the "reconfiguring" by Joseph Brodsky (1940-96) "of Vergil's pastoral poetry, specifically, the reshaping of the pastoral space in the Russian poet's eclogues" (285). Bell, Peter. Nordhausen: Traugott Bautz, 2013. Franklin, Margaret. "Vergil and the femina furens: Reading the Aeneid in Renaissance Cassone Paintings." Vergilius 60 (2014): 127-44. Houghton, L. B. T. "Virgil's Fourth Eclogue and the Visual Arts." PBSR 83 (2015): 175-220. Hudson, Hugh. "The Classical Ideal of Male Beauty in Renaissance Italy: A Note on the Afterlife of Virgil's Euryalus." JWI 76 (2013): 263-68. Pinotti, Gianna. Virgilio nella memoria contemporanea. Mantova: Paolini, 2013. Tittle, Miles. "Illuminating Divergences: Morris, Burne-Jones, and the Two Aeneids." In To Build a Shadowy Isle of Bliss: William Morris's Radicalism and the Embodi- 180 - Shirley Werner ment of Dreams, edited by Michelle Weinroth and Paul Leduc Browne, 56-84. Montreal: McGill-Queen's University Press, 2015. Vergils Aeneis in vier Zyklen von Buch-Illustrationen des 16. und 17. Jahrhunderts: Der "Venezianische Vergil" nach der Straßburger Ausgabe von 1502, der "Londoner Vergil" von 1658, der "Augsburger Vergil" von J. A. Thelot (1655-1734), der "Nürnberger Vergil" von 1688. Edited by Ulrich Wilke and Werner Suerbaum. Neukirchen: Make a Book, 2014. Bini writes about the influence of Vergil on the Italian Mannerist painter Nicolò dell'Abate (1509-71). Roethlisberger's forty-seven-page Claude Lorrain contains black-and-white and color images of the illustrations by Claude Lorrain (1600-1682) of the Iliad and Aeneid. Dimundo discusses the opera Dido and Aeneas by Henry Purcell (1659-95). Pinotti's fortypage catalog for an exhibition at the Museo Virgiliano, Pietole di Virgilio (Mantua), October 12-November 10, 2013, illustrates the work of a number of contemporary artists, mostly from the region of Mantua. Tittle discusses the unfinished Pre-Raphaelite manuscript of the Aeneid handwritten by the craftsman and designer William Morris (1834-96) and illuminated by the artist Edward Burne-Jones (1833-98). Wilke and Suerbaum present four collections of book illustrations. These include selections from Sebastian Brant's publication in Strassburg (1502) of the first large illustrated edition of Vergil's works, which contained more than two hundred woodcuts; from John Ogilby's London edition (1658), featuring over one hundred etchings; from the Aeneid printed in Nürnberg (1688) by the painter Georg Jacob Lang and the engraver Georg Christoph Eimmart, with fifty-one etchings; and the twenty-four etchings illustrating the first four books of the Aeneid created by the goldsmith Johann Andreas Thelot (1655-1734) in Augsburg. Each of the collections is also available as a separate publication. Duke University

Newburyport: Focus, 2012. 122 - Shirley Werner Gagliardi gives a history of the translation of the Eclogues into Italian from the Fascist era to the present day, observing that the comparison of translations reveals diverse approaches to poetry and to classical culture. Holzberg's Aeneis (2015) contains an introduction (9-40); Latin text and facing German translation; a note on the Latin text of this edition (675-76); a commentary (677-730); an essay, "Aeneas und Augustus," by Markus Schauer (731-59); and a bibliography (760-66). Perret's Énéide (2013) contains an introduction, Latin text and facing French translation, and notes by Paul Veyne. Trevizam's Geórgicas (2013) includes a Latin text and facing Portuguese translation. Holzberg's edition of the Eclogues and Georgics (2016) offers a Latin text and facing German translation of the poems preceded by an introduction (7-40) and followed by a note on the Latin text of this edition (255-56), a commentary (257-98), an essay, "Pascua und Rura von der Antike bis in die Gegenwart: Zum Fortwirken der Bucolica und Georgica Vergils" (299-327), and a bibliography (328-34). In their German translation and edition Leben auf dem Lande (2013), von Albrecht edited, translated, and wrote commentary for the Bucolica and Schönberger for the Georgica; the edition contains an afterword by von Albrecht. Virgile: Oeuvres complètes (2015) includes notes, a Latin text and facing French translation of the Bucoliques and Énéide by Dion and Heuzé and of the Géorgiques by Dion and Michel, and notes, Latin text, and a French translation of the Appendix Vergiliana. In Zpěvy rolnické (2016), Kurzová translates the Georgics into Czech. Ganiban's introduction to Aen. 1-6 offers a Latin text with commentary in English. Gärtner, Thomas. Gagliardi argues that the intertextual linking of Georg. 4.566 with Ecl.
1.1 indicates an ideological continuity in regard to his Augustan panegyric.
Jenkyns discusses how the concept of Rome as a city inscribed on a primeval
landscape is developed in Vergil's Georgics and books 7-8 of the Aeneid.
Laterza identifies an imperfect acrostic at Aen. 7.53-57 involving the name
Amata.

Baraz argues that Calpurnius's conception of nature as obstructive and on the importance of silence for poetic composition responds to moments of discord between man and nature in Vergil's Eclogues. Berrens discusses the influence of Aen. 1.430-36 on Seneca's Epist. 84. Dean's dissertation studies Perpetua's Passio, Proba's Cento, and Egeria's Itinerarium for what they can reveal about an educational system that enabled female authorship. Goodfellow's title quotes Martial 8.55.19. Graverini argues that in describing Lucius's movement from Greece to Rome at Met. 11.26.1 Apuleius indirectly involves Africa through a web of clues that connect Hypata, Byrrhena, and Lucius with Carthage, Dido, and Aeneas. Hinds discusses Ausonius's acknowledgement, in his secular cento, of Proba's Christian cento. Le Doze discusses Aen. 12.821-28, 834-40, and other passages. Leroux's contribution explores the treatment of Iris in Ovid and Seneca based on their source in Vergil. McCutcheon discusses the imagery of disrupted pastoral and agrarian landscapes and characters in Vergil's oeuvre and in Lucan. Power argues that Tacitus's account of Galba's end need not allude directly to Vergil's Priam. Quartarone considers the word umbra in Vergil, Ovid, Horace, and the Culex. Rudoni discusses intertextual echoes of Vergil and Lucretius in Eumolpus's poem. Schottenius Cullhed discusses Jerome, Epist. 53, where Jerome compares incompetent biblical exegesis to Vergilian and Homeric centos, with perhaps pointed reference to the work of Proba. Caldini Montanari observes that Vergil's "cadentia sidera" alludes to Sappho, fr. 168, and indicates not the end of night but midnight. Coppola points to details in the Aeneid that may reflect elements of the Alexander legend as transmitted by Lycophron; these, she argues, should be read in the context of Augustan propaganda. Fernández Corte considers the intertextual resonances of Vergil's "sus" at Aen. 8.83 with Lucretius and Horace. Gagliardi, "Alberi e amore," observes that Vergil uses tree imagery to allude to Theocritus and Gallus; in "L'Ecl. 10" she remarks on the influence of Bion, Moschus, and Gallus. Lasagna focuses on the motif of traveling upstream as it is developed in Aen. 7.36 and 8.86-101 and in Apollonius, Argonautica 2.1260-85. Pellacani argues that the syntactic ambiguity of the phrase "ab Ioue principium Musae" at Ecl. 3.60 alludes both to Theocritus 17.1 and to Cicero, Aratus, fr. 1 Soubiran, with its Hesiodic subtext. Baraz argues that Calpurnius's conception of nature as obstructive and on
the importance of silence for poetic composition responds to moments of
discord between man and nature in Vergil's Eclogues. Berrens discusses
the influence of Aen. 1.430-36 on Seneca's Epist. 84. Dean's dissertation
studies Perpetua's Passio, Proba's Cento, and Egeria's Itinerarium for what
they can reveal about an educational system that enabled female authorship.
Goodfellow's title quotes Martial 8.55.19. Graverini argues that in describing
Lucius's movement from Greece to Rome at Met. 11.26.1 Apuleius indirectly
involves Africa through a web of clues that connect Hypata, Byrrhena,
and Lucius with Carthage, Dido, and Aeneas. Hinds discusses Ausonius's
acknowledgement, in his secular cento, of Proba's Christian cento. Le
Doze discusses Aen. 12.821-28, 834-40, and other passages. Leroux's
contribution explores the treatment of Iris in Ovid and Seneca based on
their source in Vergil. McCutcheon discusses the imagery of disrupted
pastoral and agrarian landscapes and characters in Vergil's oeuvre and in
Lucan. Power argues that Tacitus's account of Galba's end need not allude
directly to Vergil's Priam. Quartarone considers the word umbra in Vergil,
Ovid, Horace, and the Culex. Rudoni discusses intertextual echoes of Vergil
and Lucretius in Eumolpus's poem. Schottenius Cullhed discusses Jerome,
Epist. 53, where Jerome compares incompetent biblical exegesis to Vergilian
and Homeric centos, with perhaps pointed reference to the work of Proba.

Caldini Montanari observes that Vergil's "cadentia sidera" alludes to Sappho,
fr. 168, and indicates not the end of night but midnight. Coppola points to
details in the Aeneid that may reflect elements of the Alexander legend as
transmitted by Lycophron; these, she argues, should be read in the context
of Augustan propaganda. Fernández Corte considers the intertextual
resonances of Vergil's "sus" at Aen. 8.83 with Lucretius and Horace.
Gagliardi, "Alberi e amore," observes that Vergil uses tree imagery to allude
to Theocritus and Gallus; in "L'Ecl. 10" she remarks on the influence of Bion,
Moschus, and Gallus. Lasagna focuses on the motif of traveling upstream as
it is developed in Aen. 7.36 and 8.86-101 and in Apollonius, Argonautica
2.1260-85. Pellacani argues that the syntactic ambiguity of the phrase "ab
Ioue principium Musae" at Ecl. 3.60 alludes both to Theocritus 17.1 and
to Cicero, Aratus, fr. 1 Soubiran, with its Hesiodic subtext. Wesolowski
discusses tree symbolism in Theocritus, Vergil, and Longus in the context
of the disappointment of personal desires.

Kiss, "Catullo," considers Servius's testimony, echoed by the humanist Guglielmo da Pastrengo (1290-1362), concerning the gender of pumex at Catullus 1.2 and concludes that, even though Servius is not reliable, it is intrinsically credible that pumex may belong to a group of nouns of the third declension in -ex or -is that are attested in both genders. In "Un passo perduto," Kiss discusses Servius's paraphrase, in his commentary on Georg. 2.95, of some polemical remarks made by Catullus concerning "uva Raetica." Kiss, "Catullo," considers Servius's testimony, echoed by the humanist
Guglielmo da Pastrengo (1290-1362), concerning the gender of pumex at
Catullus 1.2 and concludes that, even though Servius is not reliable, it is
intrinsically credible that pumex may belong to a group of nouns of the
third declension in -ex or -is that are attested in both genders. In "Un
passo perduto," Kiss discusses Servius's paraphrase, in his commentary on
Georg. 2.95, of some polemical remarks made by Catullus concerning "uva
Raetica." These words belong to no known poem and may derive from a
poem by Catullus now lost but known to Servius.

title: Ausonii e Aurunci nell'Eneide
publication_block: MEFRA 129 (2017): n.p. Simon, Erika. "Vergil und die Ara Pacis." Thetis 21 (2014): 57-62. Torelli, Mario. "L'Etruria di Virgilio: Immaginario e realtà." MEFRA 129 (2017): n.p. Cugusi discusses the heterogeneous character of twenty-eight inscriptional poems from Roman Britain dating from the first through sixth centuries, some of which contain Vergilian echoes. Goldschmidt's paper focuses on the ways in which Horace, Vergil, and Livy responded to Carthage and the Punic Wars. Guittard contemplates the concept of the Augustan return of the Golden Age in Vergil's Eclogues, Georgics, and Aeneid. Nelis studies the ways in which the names Maecenas, Vergilius, and Caesar function within the Georgics and how they illuminate the relationship between the poem and its historical context. Pagliara untangles the complex literary tradition concerning the Ausones of ancient Italy: the ethnic Ausones are mentioned in historical and geographical sources on Campania; but in Greek poetry Ausonia occurs simply as a synonym for Italy. Vergil first uses Ausonii of the peoples allied with Turnus, then extends the term to all the people included in Aeneas's pax, "thus foreshadowing the future pax Augusta; finally, with a natural shift, he applies it to the Romans, who inherit all that world." Thus "Virgil inserts in his erudite reconstruction of the ancient peopling Vergilian Bibliography - 167 of Italy the old Greek notion of Ausones, which he borrowed in its usefully adaptable semantic vagueness from his Hellenistic poetic sources." Simon uses the role of Juno in the Aeneid to interpret the sacrificial relief depicting Aeneas on the Ara Pacis. The relief refers not to a sacrifice to the Penates in Lavinium but to a sacrifice to Juno on the Tiber. Capdeville, Gérard. "Diomede ed Antenore, rivali letterari ed ideologici di Enea." Cugusi discusses the heterogeneous character of twenty-eight inscriptional poems from Roman Britain dating from the first through sixth centuries, some of which contain Vergilian echoes. Guittard contemplates the concept of the Augustan return of the Golden Age in Vergil's Eclogues, Georgics, and Aeneid. Nelis studies the ways in which the names Maecenas, Vergilius, and Caesar function within the Georgics and how they illuminate the relationship between the poem and its historical context. Pagliara untangles the complex literary tradition concerning the Ausones of ancient Italy: the ethnic Ausones are mentioned in historical and geographical sources on Campania; but in Greek poetry Ausonia occurs simply as a synonym for Italy. Vergil first uses Ausonii of the peoples allied with Turnus, then extends the term to all the people included in Aeneas's pax, "thus foreshadowing the future pax Augusta; finally, with a natural shift, he applies it to the Romans, who inherit all that world." Thus "Virgil inserts in his erudite reconstruction of the ancient peopling Vergilian Bibliography - 167 of Italy the old Greek notion of Ausones, which he borrowed in its usefully adaptable semantic vagueness from his Hellenistic poetic sources." Simon uses the role of Juno in the Aeneid to interpret the sacrificial relief depicting Aeneas on the Ara Pacis. Capdeville discusses two epic poems by poets contemporary with Vergil who took different approaches to legendary Roman history: Iullus Antonius, son of M. Davis "reflects on the changing nature of free speech in the Augustan period" through an examination of the council of the Latins in Aen. 11, in which "Drances can demand freedom of speech in a public context," and "a sequence of stories in Metamorphoses 2 and 3 in which outspokenness is punished. … This reflects, I suggest, the altered political circumstances between the 20s BCE and the first decade CE" (183). Burghini and Uría draw attention to Consentius's discussion, in De barbarismis et metaplasmis, of vowel shortening at Aen. 3.602 (scio) and Georg. 1.232 (duodena). Feeney considers the first similes in Homer's Iliad and Odyssey, Lucretius's De rerum natura, and Vergil's Aeneid, all of which introduce narrative techniques and themes that carry through the poem as a whole. Fontaine argues that "several puns in the poetry of Virgil, Lucretius, and Catullus … are better explained by a psycholinguistic than a psychoanalytic (Freudian) model. Since they appear in correct, ordinary speech-in contrast to Freudian slips, in which a pun results from a speech error-I call those puns 'Freudian bullseyes'" (131). Foster argues that Servius's commentaries on Vergil provide insight into
what might have happened in his classroom. His teaching had to enable
students who did not all speak Latin as a native language to recognize
both archaic and contemporary Latin and to distinguish between different
formal registers, including prose and verse. Lunelli discusses a Vergilian
scholion in the margin of Verona, Biblioteca Capitolare, MS XL (olim 38),
fol. 253r, with its controversial claim for the existence of a cult of Apollo
near Padua. Peirano considers how ancient authorial ascriptions function
as paratexts, claiming authority for the text even when that authority may
sometimes be faked. Smith observes that Cato is cited by Servius more than
any other historian, "and looked at the other way round, Servius is a very
important source for our knowledge of Cato." His study asks "what we learn
Vergilian Bibliography - 175
from Servius' use of Cato, and what we learn about Virgil." Squillante writes
that Vergil's favorable depiction of the cities of Italy was a way of honoring
the Augustan program. This perspective influenced later Latin literature
from Pliny to Claudian to Namatianus and is amplified in Servius and other
late antique commentaries in the context of the revival of imperial ideology
in the late fourth and early fifth centuries. Stok's goal is to reconstruct
the Vergilian text commented on by Servius Danielis (DS). He considers
instances where DS proposes a lemma different from that of Servius;
lemmas in DS not commented on by Servius; and cases in which DS reports
variants of Vergil's transmitted text.

Bernabò, in a reassessment that is "entirely an art historian investigation,"
discusses the Vergilius Romanus (Rome, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana,
cod. Vat. Lat. 3867. "Unconvinced of the claims that the manuscript was
produced either in Italy (i.e., Rome or Ravenna) or Constantinople, I argue
for an origin in a Western province of the Roman Empire, probably southern
Gaul" (239). Dickey discusses "bilingual papyrus texts of Vergil and Cicero,
with the Latin original and a Greek translation arranged in distinctive
narrow columns," which provide "a unique window" into the experience
of nonnative Latin speakers learning to read the classics of Latin literature.
This system of exegesis "was fundamental to ancient learning" but has "been
overlooked and misunderstood in modern times, in part because it has no
parallel in modern teaching or scholarship" (807). Columnar translation
probably developed from columnar glossaries. Fusi endorses Heinsius's
emendation of "variisque" to "Phariisque" at Aen. 8.685. Huyck clarifies
Turnus's furious speech to his men in Aen. 9, who are shaken after having
witnessed the transformation of the Trojan ships, by emendation. "The
difficulty of construing the words penitus … / femineum (141-142), whether

Ciampolo di Meo degli Ugurgieri, the subject of Lagomarsini's study, was
the first to translate the Aeneid into Italian. O'Sullivan finds in the glosses
of the ninth-century Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Auct. F.2.8 "a strong
endorsement of pagan learning" and an interest in encyclopedism. The
glosses show "that it was not only Vergil's style but also his content that
engaged Christian readers." This conclusion "challenges the older view of
confrontation between pagan and Christian cultures."

Alonso Fernández reads the libretto of La Didone, a coreodramma (dramatic
ballet) by the Italian choreographer and composer Salvatore Viganò (1769-
1821) that reconfigured the Dido story in Aen. 1 and 4, with attention to
the corporeal and kinaesthetic potential in Vergil's story. She argues that
"these traces of bodily movement and expressivity were motivated by the …
performance culture in Augustan times, in particular, by the growing trend
of pantomime dancing" (400). Eastin interprets the visual anachronims in
the first illustrated print edition of Vergil's works, "published in Strasbourg
in 1502 under the direction of the German humanist Sebastian Brant"
(1457-1521). The anachronisms "serve as visual translations that capture
the greater message of Virgil's poetry and so help to interpret the social,
political, and artistic concerns of both Virgil and Brant" (85). Gesztelyi
demonstrates that the Julio-Claudian Sebasteion built in Aphrodisias
contains reliefs depicting scenes from the Aeneid that show iconographic
parallels with some of the illustrations in Vatican City, Biblioteca Apostolica
Vaticana, MS Vat. lat. 3225. Heslin argues that the poetry of Vergil, Horace,
and Propertius was influenced by paintings of the Trojan War. Venturini
compares a fourth-century mosaic depicting Aeneas and Turnus from the
House of Medusa at Alter do Chão, Portugal, with Aen. 12.919-52, the
passage it illustrates.

Davies discusses the reception of the magical and prophetic Vergil of popular legend and the place of the Eclogues, Georgics, and Aeneid in Wales from the Middle Ages on. Florano and Zorzetti discuss Giovanni Boccaccio (1313-75). Iafrate touches on the medieval literary persona of Vergil as a magician who could capture demons in a bottle. Martin "focuses on Heinrich von Veldeke's Eneasroman (ca. 1186), the first courtly romance written in German. Perucchi discusses Francesco Petrarch (1304-74). Ratkowitsch demonstrates that the Latin poem Waltharius depends on the Aeneid for individual motifs and overall structure. Ricklin discusses the influence of the medieval legends about Vergil on William of Malmesbury's (1080-1143) depiction of Sylvester II as a necromantic philosopher. Abbamonte observes that the humanist Giuniano Maio (1430-93) derived his quotations from Vergil from second-hand sources (Servius, Nonius, Gellius, and others). Auger discusses Cristoforo Landino (1424-98). Basu considers the Arcadia of Philip Sidney (1554-86) as a form of fan fiction of the Eclogues. Béhar discusses the influence of Eurydice in Georg. 4 on the character of Elisa in the third Eclogue of Garcilaso de la Vega (1501- 36). Cappelli analyzes quotations from Vergil in Neapolitan political treatises by Giovanni Pontano (1426-1503), Francesco Patrizi (1529-97), and Giuniano Maio. Casanova-Robin considers the meaning behind the etiological tale about Vergil's birth devised by Pontano in elegy 1.14 of his Eridanus. Deramaix discusses the imitation of Vergil in the writings of Jacopo Sannazaro (1458-1530). Escobar Borrego writes about the concept of Vergil's Arcadia and its influence on Don Alonso Pérez de Guzmán (1550-1615) and suggests that Soledades and Retrato by his son Pedro Espinosa (1578-1650) are best understood in that context. Disagreeing with twentieth-century views that take Lucan's Bellum civile as a parodic subversion of Vergil, Facchini argues that Dante regards the epics of Vergil and Lucan as consonant with each other. Ferreri offers reflections on sources pertaining to the Vergilian exegesis of Aulo Giano Parrasio (Aulus Janus Parrhasius) (1470-1522) in order to focus on his commentaries on Horace's Ars poetica, on Claudianus's De raptu Proserpinae, and on his own De rebus per epistolam quaesitis. Foley compares Cristoforo Landino's (1424-98) interpretation of the Aeneid, which he read as a moral allegory of the soul's progression to philosophical wisdom, with the similar interpretive scheme that Marsilio Ficino (1433-99) applied to the dialogues of Plato. Gevrey discusses Les exilés de la cour d'Auguste by Marie-Catherine de Villedieu (1640-83). Hampton writes about Os Lusíadas by Luís de Camões (1524-80). Holmes notes that the embrace between Vergil and Sordello, a thirteenth-century troubadour born, like Vergil, in Mantua, "binds with Vergilian Bibliography - 205 love … all of Italy's previously scattered languages, literary genres, and political ideologies" (106). Houghton, "Virgil," considers "the contribution made by poetic imagery derived from the work of Virgil," in particular from "the fourth Eclogue, to contemporary formulations of the concept of the Renaissance itself" (203). Laterza considers Vergil's use of pathetic fallacy in the cave scene in Aen. 4 and how it was reworked in Renaissance literature. Lovatt explores the use of Vergil's underworld and the reworking of Dante in Renaissance Florence by Ugolino Verino (1438-1516) in his epic poem Carlias. MacCarthy's essay focuses on the "poet-musician Iopas [at Aen. 1.740-47], the subject of his banquet song and the increased interest in him as a musician and poet by late medieval and early Renaissance writers" (85-86). Marshall writes that in 1592 "Robert Greene warned his fellow playwrights that 'there is an upstart Crow, beautified with our feathers, that with his Tigers hart wrapped in a players hyde, supposes he is as well able to blast out a blank verse as the best of us." Greene almost certainly meant William Shakespeare as the upstart crow, and drew the image from Vergil's cornix … improba at Georg. 1.388-89, which he must have known in Latin. Marsico writes on Lorenzo Valla (1407-57). Martín Romano writes about the major influence of the Aeneid on the epic poem Nuevo Mundo y conquista by Francisco de Terrazas (1540-1600), the first Mexican poet. Martindale considers the period from Henry Howard, Earl of Surrey (1517-47) to Alfred, Lord Tennyson (1809-92). Mauriello takes Arcadia and Eclogae piscatoriae by Sannazaro as a starting point to examine vernacular bucolic in Italy during the sixteenth century, focusing ultimately on the poetry of Luigi Tansillo (1510-68) and Berardino Rota (1508-75). Michon discusses Pierre-Valentin Faydit's (1644-1709) reception of Vergil. Miletti observes that an elegy by Francesco Peto da Fondi (1480?-after 1531), following Servius's commentary, offers an antiquarian inquiry on Amyclae, the Spartan colony in southern Latium mentioned at Aen. 10.564. Molins discusses Joachim Du Bellay (1522-60). Mussio maintains that Dante's interpretation of Vergil's Amata in Purg. 17 as an example of wrath is consistent with his treatment of Amata in Epist. 7. Nassichuk focuses on an elegiac poem by Nicolas Chesneau (1521-81). Pérez-Abadin Barro discusses the Églogas pastoriles, modeled on Vergil's Eclogues, by Pedro de Padilla (1540-99). Pietras discusses The Shepheardes Calender by Edmund Spenser (1552-99). Potter writes on Christopher Marlowe (1564-93). Pugh focuses on Edmund Spenser's (1552-99) acknowledged debt in his pastoral poems to Vergil's Eclogues. Quint writes on Angelo Ambrogini (Poliziano) (1454-94). Reeser discusses Michel de Montaigne (1533-92). Rogerson writes about Maffeo Vegio's 1428 Supplement to the Aeneid, the "extraordinarily successful 'Aeneid 13'" (119). Wingfield examines the 206 - Shirley Werner correspondences that might be drawn between a set of extracts from Julius Caesar Scaliger's (1494-1558) Epidorpides (published posthumously in 1573) copied in the Ruthven manuscript (Edinburgh, University Library, MS Dc.1.43) and the subsequent translation of Virgil's Aeneid by Gavin Douglas (1474-1522). Séris writes on the neo-Latin poet Girolamo Angeriano (Hieronymus Angerianus, 1480-1512). Toscano writes about six eclogues by Giano Anisio (1465-1640) in his Varia poemata that blend Vergilian and contemporary influences. Tucker's subject is the sixteenth- to seventeenth-century Latin verse cento based preponderantly upon Vergil. Tufano considers a description of a firework display by Gabriele Altilio (1436-1501) that is modeled on Vergil's description of the burning of Troy. Calvert, Ian. O'Briain takes Dryden's 1697 translation of the Georgics as a case study to recover "a common history of georgic and fictionality that resides in [Vergil's and Dryden's] shared attention to craft labour" (317). Sjödin uses Bakhtin's theory of the mémoire du genre to study the influence of the Saisons by JeanFrançois de Saint-Lambert (1716-1803) in the work of the Swedish poet Johan Gabriel Oxenstierna (1750-1818), both of which stand as examples of Vergilian-inspired georgics. Frost considers the question whether death provides a generative force in politics and points to the opinion of Hannah Arendt (1906-75), who felt that birth, not death, equipped us for the task of beginning and that the Aeneid shows the best understanding of this process. Marshall considers the place of Vergil in the poetic imagination of Tony Harrison (1937-). Casali discusses the Nisus and Euryalus episode in the Aeneid; the nocturnal massacre in Statius, Th. 10; and the Doloneia in Silius Italicus's Punica. Duval observes that ancient evidence for the Sortes Vergilianae is attested only in the Historia Augusta, whereas the Sortes Homericae are witnessed in three papyri. Fratantuono notices that the first book of Quintus of Smyrna's Posthomerica manipulates and reverses the plot of Aen. 11. Houghton cites Tibullus 1.3.35-50, Horace, Carm. saec. 57-60 and Carm. 4.2.37-40, and passages from Vergil himself that demonstrate early responses to his fourth Eclogue. López Romero notes that Seneca's maiora notis monstra alludes to nota maior imago at Aen. 2.773. Seidman observes that Tacitus's approach to the Teutoburg episode has much in common with Aeneas's viewing of the Trojan War mural at the Temple of Juno in Carthage (Aen. 1.451-65). Ware focuses on the fourth-century corpus of the Panegyrici Latini, whose orators "acknowledge Virgil as magnus poeta and cite him directly, particularly to evoke battle scenes, while also drawing on his work generally for poetic language and epic phrasing" (1). Farley studies adaptations of Homer in order to make a scholarly distinction between the work of fan writers and the work of rewriters, such as Vergil and Alexander Pope. Fratantuono juxtaposes Aen. 6.125 and Hesiod, Th. 154 and following. Mellado Rodríguez examines parallels between Aen. 4.9-53 and Euripides's Hippolytus. O'Rourke observes that the intertextual relationship between reception narratives in the Aeneid (Aeneas and Evander) and Callimachus's Aetia (Heracles and Molorcus) and Hecale (Theseus and Hecale) are significant. Lagioia, with a view to a new edition of Servius's commentary, examines the first book of the Georgics to provide critical reflection on the edition of G. Thilo (1881-87) and on the criteria to be adopted for a new text. Pirovano's is the second part of a study of the manuscripts Bern, Burgerbibliothek 165 (T), and Vatican, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, Vat. Lat. 1570 (F). Racine discusses the role of Greek in Servius's commentary and in the Roman education and culture of his day. Murgia and Kaster's edition is the first addition to the Harvard Servius to appear in over fifty years. Stok speculates that the word virgineum in the context of an acrostic at Georg. 1.430 may have suggested his ancient nickname Parthenias that, according to Donatus in his Vita Vergili, was assigned to Vergil in Naples. Urîa finds a previously undetected connection between the earliest approaches to what is known Vergilian Bibliography - 199 as the septimus casus and those found in Servius's commentaries on Vergil, approaches that differ from those followed in the later grammarians. Vallat (2017) analyzes the transmission of Varro in late antique Vergil commentaries, discussing the reliability of authors' names and titles of works in citations, forms of quotation, complications entailed by manuscript transmission, and other issues.
