Appleton and Robinson write on the Anglo-Latin hagiography Vita Sancti Guthlaci, concerning the hermit Guthlac of Crowland (d. 714), written by a certain Felix ca. 730-40. Davies discusses the reception of the magical and prophetic Vergil of popular legend and the place of the Eclogues, Georgics, and Aeneid in Wales from the Middle Ages on. Florano and Zorzetti discuss Giovanni Boccaccio (1313-75). Iafrate touches on the medieval literary persona of Vergil as a magician who could capture demons in a bottle. Ibáñez Chacón discusses the Liber monstrorum, a late seventh- or early eighthcentury catalog of marvelous creatures whose mythological narratives are drawn largely from Vergil. Martin "focuses on Heinrich von Veldeke's Eneasroman (ca. 1186), the first courtly romance written in German. It is an adaptation of the Old French Roman d'Eneas (c. 1160), itself an adaptation of Vergil's Aeneid" (18). O'Sullivan discusses Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Auct. F.2.8. Perucchi discusses Francesco Petrarch (1304-74). Ratkowitsch demonstrates that the Latin poem Waltharius depends on the Aeneid for individual motifs and overall structure. Ricklin discusses the influence of the medieval legends about Vergil on William of Malmesbury's (1080-1143) depiction of Sylvester II as a necromantic philosopher. Abbamonte observes that the humanist Giuniano Maio (1430-93) derived his quotations from Vergil from second-hand sources (Servius, Nonius, Gellius, and others). Auger discusses Cristoforo Landino (1424-98). Basu considers the Arcadia of Philip Sidney (1554-86) as a form of fan fiction of the Eclogues. Béhar discusses the influence of Eurydice in Georg. 4 on the character of Elisa in the third Eclogue of Garcilaso de la Vega (1501- 36). Cappelli analyzes quotations from Vergil in Neapolitan political treatises by Giovanni Pontano (1426-1503), Francesco Patrizi (1529-97), and Giuniano Maio. Casanova-Robin considers the meaning behind the etiological tale about Vergil's birth devised by Pontano in elegy 1.14 of his Eridanus. Deramaix discusses the imitation of Vergil in the writings of Jacopo Sannazaro (1458-1530). Escobar Borrego writes about the concept of Vergil's Arcadia and its influence on Don Alonso Pérez de Guzmán (1550-1615) and suggests that Soledades and Retrato by his son Pedro Espinosa (1578-1650) are best understood in that context. Disagreeing with twentieth-century views that take Lucan's Bellum civile as a parodic subversion of Vergil, Facchini argues that Dante regards the epics of Vergil and Lucan as consonant with each other. Ferreri offers reflections on sources pertaining to the Vergilian exegesis of Aulo Giano Parrasio (Aulus Janus Parrhasius) (1470-1522) in order to focus on his commentaries on Horace's Ars poetica, on Claudianus's De raptu Proserpinae, and on his own De rebus per epistolam quaesitis. Foley compares Cristoforo Landino's (1424-98) interpretation of the Aeneid, which he read as a moral allegory of the soul's progression to philosophical wisdom, with the similar interpretive scheme that Marsilio Ficino (1433-99) applied to the dialogues of Plato. Gevrey discusses Les exilés de la cour d'Auguste by Marie-Catherine de Villedieu (1640-83). Hampton writes about Os Lusíadas by Luís de Camões (1524-80). Holmes notes that the embrace between Vergil and Sordello, a thirteenth-century troubadour born, like Vergil, in Mantua, "binds with Vergilian Bibliography - 205 love … all of Italy's previously scattered languages, literary genres, and political ideologies" (106). Houghton, "Virgil," considers "the contribution made by poetic imagery derived from the work of Virgil," in particular from "the fourth Eclogue, to contemporary formulations of the concept of the Renaissance itself" (203). Laterza considers Vergil's use of pathetic fallacy in the cave scene in Aen. 4 and how it was reworked in Renaissance literature. Lovatt explores the use of Vergil's underworld and the reworking of Dante in Renaissance Florence by Ugolino Verino (1438-1516) in his epic poem Carlias. MacCarthy's essay focuses on the "poet-musician Iopas [at Aen. 1.740-47], the subject of his banquet song and the increased interest in him as a musician and poet by late medieval and early Renaissance writers" (85-86). Marshall writes that in 1592 "Robert Greene warned his fellow playwrights that 'there is an upstart Crow, beautified with our feathers, that with his Tigers hart wrapped in a players hyde, supposes he is as well able to blast out a blank verse as the best of us." Greene almost certainly meant William Shakespeare as the upstart crow, and drew the image from Vergil's cornix … improba at Georg. 1.388-89, which he must have known in Latin. Marsico writes on Lorenzo Valla (1407-57). Martín Romano writes about the major influence of the Aeneid on the epic poem Nuevo Mundo y conquista by Francisco de Terrazas (1540-1600), the first Mexican poet. Martindale considers the period from Henry Howard, Earl of Surrey (1517-47) to Alfred, Lord Tennyson (1809-92). Mauriello takes Arcadia and Eclogae piscatoriae by Sannazaro as a starting point to examine vernacular bucolic in Italy during the sixteenth century, focusing ultimately on the poetry of Luigi Tansillo (1510-68) and Berardino Rota (1508-75). Michon discusses Pierre-Valentin Faydit's (1644-1709) reception of Vergil. Miletti observes that an elegy by Francesco Peto da Fondi (1480?-after 1531), following Servius's commentary, offers an antiquarian inquiry on Amyclae, the Spartan colony in southern Latium mentioned at Aen. 10.564. only in the Historia Augusta, whereas the Sortes Homericae are witnessed in
three papyri. Fratantuono notices that the first book of Quintus of Smyrna's
Posthomerica manipulates and reverses the plot of Aen. 11. Houghton cites

Lagioia, with a view to a new edition of Servius's commentary, examines the
first book of the Georgics to provide critical reflection on the edition of G.
Thilo (1881-87) and on the criteria to be adopted for a new text. Pirovano's
is the second part of a study of the manuscripts Bern, Burgerbibliothek 165
(T), and Vatican, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, Vat. Lat. 1570 (F). Racine
discusses the role of Greek in Servius's commentary and in the Roman
education and culture of his day. Murgia and Kaster's edition is the first
addition to the Harvard Servius to appear in over fifty years. Stok speculates
that the word virgineum in the context of an acrostic at Georg. 1.430 may
have suggested his ancient nickname Parthenias that, according to Donatus
in his Vita Vergili, was assigned to Vergil in Naples. Urîa finds a previously
undetected connection between the earliest approaches to what is known

Abbamonte observes that the humanist Giuniano Maio (1430-93) derived
his quotations from Vergil from second-hand sources (Servius, Nonius,
Gellius, and others). Auger discusses Cristoforo Landino (1424-98). Basu
considers the Arcadia of Philip Sidney (1554-86) as a form of fan fiction
of the Eclogues. Béhar discusses the influence of Eurydice in Georg. 4 on
the character of Elisa in the third Eclogue of Garcilaso de la Vega (1501-
36). Cappelli analyzes quotations from Vergil in Neapolitan political
treatises by Giovanni Pontano (1426-1503), Francesco Patrizi (1529-97),
and Giuniano Maio. Casanova-Robin considers the meaning behind the
etiological tale about Vergil's birth devised by Pontano in elegy 1.14 of his
Eridanus. Deramaix discusses the imitation of Vergil in the writings of
Jacopo Sannazaro (1458-1530). Escobar Borrego writes about the concept
of Vergil's Arcadia and its influence on Don Alonso Pérez de Guzmán
(1550-1615) and suggests that Soledades and Retrato by his son Pedro
Espinosa (1578-1650) are best understood in that context. Disagreeing
with twentieth-century views that take Lucan's Bellum civile as a parodic
subversion of Vergil, Facchini argues that Dante regards the epics of Vergil
and Lucan as consonant with each other. Ferreri offers reflections on
sources pertaining to the Vergilian exegesis of Aulo Giano Parrasio (Aulus
Janus Parrhasius) (1470-1522) in order to focus on his commentaries on
Horace's Ars poetica, on Claudianus's De raptu Proserpinae, and on his
own De rebus per epistolam quaesitis. Foley compares Cristoforo Landino's
(1424-98) interpretation of the Aeneid, which he read as a moral allegory of
the soul's progression to philosophical wisdom, with the similar interpretive
scheme that Marsilio Ficino (1433-99) applied to the dialogues of Plato.
Gevrey discusses Les exilés de la cour d'Auguste by Marie-Catherine de
Villedieu (1640-83). Hampton writes about Os Lusíadas by Luís de Camões
(1524-80). Holmes notes that the embrace between Vergil and Sordello, a
thirteenth-century troubadour born, like Vergil, in Mantua, "binds with
Vergilian Bibliography - 205
love … all of Italy's previously scattered languages, literary genres, and
political ideologies" (106). Houghton, "Virgil," considers "the contribution
made by poetic imagery derived from the work of Virgil," in particular from
"the fourth Eclogue, to contemporary formulations of the concept of the
Renaissance itself" (203). Laterza considers Vergil's use of pathetic fallacy in
the cave scene in Aen. 4 and how it was reworked in Renaissance literature.
Lovatt explores the use of Vergil's underworld and the reworking of Dante
in Renaissance Florence by Ugolino Verino (1438-1516) in his epic poem
Carlias. MacCarthy's essay focuses on the "poet-musician Iopas [at Aen.
1.740-47], the subject of his banquet song and the increased interest
in him as a musician and poet by late medieval and early Renaissance
writers" (85-86). Marshall writes that in 1592 "Robert Greene warned
his fellow playwrights that 'there is an upstart Crow, beautified with our
feathers, that with his Tigers hart wrapped in a players hyde, supposes he
is as well able to blast out a blank verse as the best of us." Greene almost
certainly meant William Shakespeare as the upstart crow, and drew the
image from Vergil's cornix … improba at Georg. 1.388-89, which he must
have known in Latin. Marsico writes on Lorenzo Valla (1407-57). Martín
Romano writes about the major influence of the Aeneid on the epic poem
Nuevo Mundo y conquista by Francisco de Terrazas (1540-1600), the first
Mexican poet. Martindale considers the period from Henry Howard, Earl
of Surrey (1517-47) to Alfred, Lord Tennyson (1809-92). Mauriello takes
Arcadia and Eclogae piscatoriae by Sannazaro as a starting point to examine
vernacular bucolic in Italy during the sixteenth century, focusing ultimately
on the poetry of Luigi Tansillo (1510-68) and Berardino Rota (1508-75).
Michon discusses Pierre-Valentin Faydit's (1644-1709) reception of Vergil.
Miletti observes that an elegy by Francesco Peto da Fondi (1480?-after
1531), following Servius's commentary, offers an antiquarian inquiry on
Amyclae, the Spartan colony in southern Latium mentioned at Aen. 10.564.
Molins discusses Joachim Du Bellay (1522-60). Mussio maintains that
Dante's interpretation of Vergil's Amata in Purg. 17 as an example of wrath
is consistent with his treatment of Amata in Epist. 7. Nassichuk focuses
on an elegiac poem by Nicolas Chesneau (1521-81). Pérez-Abadin Barro
discusses the Églogas pastoriles, modeled on Vergil's Eclogues, by Pedro de
Padilla (1540-99). Pietras discusses The Shepheardes Calender by Edmund
Spenser (1552-99). Potter writes on Christopher Marlowe (1564-93).
Pugh focuses on Edmund Spenser's (1552-99) acknowledged debt in his
pastoral poems to Vergil's Eclogues. Quint writes on Angelo Ambrogini
(Poliziano) (1454-94). Reeser discusses Michel de Montaigne (1533-92).
Rogerson writes about Maffeo Vegio's 1428 Supplement to the Aeneid, the
"extraordinarily successful 'Aeneid 13'" (119). Wingfield examines the

Frick discusses the 1543 Worms edition of the Aeneid translation by Thomas Murner (1475-1537) into German, which included frontispieces that represented the content of each book visually and can only be understood by a reader who knows the entire Aeneid. Maass considers the art nouveau of Adolphe Giraldon (1855-1933) and the influence of Ecl. 1 and 4. Nollé writes about a medallion coined in Antandros in the Troad during the reign of Severus Alexandrinus that depicts the flight of Aeneas from Troy with Anchises and Ascanius. Vitela considers the maiolica dishes painted by Nicola da Urbino (active ca. 1520-1537/8) that were illustrated with classical narratives, including scenes inspired by the Aeneid and Ovid's Metamorphoses. Duke University

Frick considers the first translation of the Aeneid into German, by Thomas
Murner (Strasbourg, 1515); and the later translation by Johannes Spreng,
published posthumously in 1610. Heuzé discusses challenges encountered
by translators of the Aeneid. After noting the influence of Johann Heinrich
Voß's translation of Homer on metrical translations of Latin poetry,
Holzberg argues for the possibility of a metrical translation of the Aeneid that
avoids artificial and archaizing language. Paschalis discusses Renaissance
humanists' translations of Vergil's Eclogues into Doric Greek hexameters,
imitating the poetic style of Theocritus; and their translations of Theocritus
into Latin prose or, less commonly, into Vergilian-style Latin hexameters.
Homer was also translated into Vergilian hexameters. Romani Mistretta
discusses the Latin text, French translation, notes, and illustrations of the
Aeneid edited by Jacques Delille (1738-1813).

Vergil's purple sheep in Ecl. 4. The color is a literary symbol for the poetic
difficulties in comparing very different worlds and phases of time.

In composing his Cento nuptialis, writes Budaragina, Ausonius allows
himself to borrow a maximum of one and a half contiguous lines from Vergil's
text. The text at praef. 26-27 is thus seriously corrupt and should be marked
with a crux: "unus † et sequens cum medio …". The Cento Tityri edited by
Canellis is an erudite Christian eclogue based on Vergil's verse, put in the
form of a dialogue and drawing on various genres. Clément-Tarantino and
Klein write that the cento Europa uses fragments of Vergil's verse but derives
its subject matter from Ovid, Metam. 2. It thus paradoxically represents a
Vergilian rereading of Ovid. Fels and Greschat offer a translation of and
commentary on Proba's Cento. Kochenash suggests that the mention of
Tabitha at Acts 9.36-43 immediately after Peter's healing of Aeneas can be
read as an allusion to Vergil's Dido, since Aramaic Tabitha and Greek Dorcas
(the translation of her name) both mean "deer" and Dido is characterized
as a deer in the Aeneid. Marin (2017b) observes that Jerome, Epist. 30.13.2,
alludes to Georg. 2.506. Tautschnig discusses Seneca's allusion in his Epist.
21 to the Nisus and Euryalus episode. Vallat analyzes a database of Vergilian
centones to discover which passages were best known in late antiquity, and
offers in an appendix a list of the Vergilian verses cited three or more times.
Wasyl's edition includes the Latin text of two poems preserved in Paris,
Bibliothèque nationale de France, MS lat. 10318. The cento Alcestis, inspired
by Vergil, bears witness to literary production in North Africa under the
Vandals.

audiences, "and that is why its continuing influence on how we read Vergil is

Ammirati provides a palaeographical and codicological description of a

inv. 1931.20.002, the only extant evidence of a text of Vergil (Aen. 3.136-

minuscule, with numerous interlinear and marginal glosses. Berti offers an
extensive review of E. Kraggerud, Vergiliana (2016). Delvigo discusses the
earliest defensores and obtrectatores of Vergil's text. Fressura investigates the
circumstances under which fols. 115-16 of Milan, Biblioteca Ambrosiana di
Milano, MS L 120, were lost. The bifolium was a palimpsest containing, in
its scriptio inferior, a portion of the Aeneid with a Greek translation datable
to the sixth century. Hendrickson writes on ancient books purporting to
Vergilian Bibliography - 145
be autograph manuscripts by Vergil. Luzzatto argues that the reading
Diomeden attested at Aen. 11.243 in the older manuscripts and in Servius's
and Macrobius's commentaries is not an error but testifies to the original
spelling of an Augustan edition. Pecere dates Florence, Biblioteca Medicea
Laurenziana, MS Plut. 39.1 to the last years of the fifth or beginning of the
sixth century. Scappaticcio (2016) considers the papyrological evidence
for the circulation of the Aeneid from the first through sixth centuries.
Scappaticcio (2017) writes that in Egypt the widespread study of Latin as
a second language is already observable in papyri of the first century BC,
where Vergil's work especially was considered suitable for exercitationes
scribendi. Schafer argues that the Eclogues and Georgics can meaningfully
be considered in terms of columns of script (paginae), 36 lines per column
for the former and 40 for the latter; the text mimetically comments on its
column breaks. Shumilin proposes reading longe in place of the transmitted
longa at Aen. 12.510, comparing Il. 20.460-62 and Statius, Theb. 9.108.

finds direct allusions to the Aeneid's plot and characters as part of a broader
commentary on Arthur's kingship. Smolak writes about the medieval
Christianization of Vergil and Ovid that was accomplished through forging
new texts under their names.

Johnson observes that Gavin Douglas (ca. 1476-1522) regarded himself as
a philological humanist "following in the footsteps of Laurentius Valla." Yet
Douglas's reliance on Christian commentary in his translation of Vergil's
Aeneid, his Christianizing of his pagan source, and his textual attentiveness
"show not only affinities with the late medieval tradition of learned translation
but also with the high-order humanism of a scholastic literary sensibility"
(71). Laird reflects on the late antique constructions of Vergil's character,
which led to a more vivid impression of his presence, and on the reception
of this tradition with its implicit tensions in early modern authors, such as
Petrarch. Lanzarone offers observations on the unpublished commentary
by the Italian scholar Giulio Pomponio Leto (ca. 1428-98) on the Appendix
Vergiliana. The commentary contains antiquarian, geographical, historical,
and mythological notes. Maronese focuses especially on Dante. Aeneas
Silvius Piccolomini's choice of the name Pius when he was elected pope in
1458, thereby becoming Pope Pius II, raises questions about his reception of
Vergil, Stok writes. Cristoforo Landino (1424-98) is the subject of Taufer's
study. Whittington discusses the "thirteenth book of the Aeneid" composed
by the Italian humanist Maffeo Vegio (1407-58).

Coffee uses digital methods to address "the phenomenon of 'viral inter
textuality,' or instances of distinct language that appear serially over
multiple literary works" (177), using as his example Vergil's description
of Lavinia's blush at Aen. 12, 67-69. In so doing he discovers "instances
where the phrase seems to pass beyond the realm of poetic imitation, or
even generic language, to become a constellation of ideas and images that
148 - Shirley Werner
float free into the thought-world drawn on by later poets and even prose
authors. Considering this wide swath of textual relationships together
brings us to a model of intertextuality different from the common one"
(178). Gurd analyzes Xenotext One (2015), a collection of poems and
theoretical statements by Christian Bök, which contains a translation of
Georg. 1.

Nolfo is concerned with a Latin epigram, wrongly attributed to Ausonius
and preserved in the fourth- or fifth-century Epigrammata Bobiensia,
which translates a Greek epigram (AP 151) that presents itself as a first-
person utterance by an artistic image of Dido. Pavan discusses the gallery
of paintings by various artists of the baroque era illustrating scenes from
the Aeneid collected in the Palazzo Buonaccorsi. Putnam interprets Pietro
da Cortona's baroque frescoes, depicting figures from the Aeneid, created
between 1651-55 for the Palazzo Pamphilj on the Piazza Navona in Rome.
Scalera writes about the seventeenth-century tragicomic opera Dido by
the librettist Giovanni Francesco Busenello (1598-1659) and composer
Francesco Cavalli (1602-76), which ends with a wedding between Dido and
Iarbas. Torre gives an overview of Italian versions of the Aeneid published

in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries and of their illustrations, which
can be considered a source for the artistic imagery on display in the Palazzo
Bonaccorsi.

La Barbera 2019a discusses editors' suggestions for emendation of et
laeta at Culex 140 and recommends the emendations seque ipsae extendunt
at 143 and non inscia fati at 145. La Barbera 2019b emends cubuere at Culex
154 to accubuere; and at 156 suggests pergit for the transmitted poscit and,
more tentatively, susurranti vento for the transmitted susurrantis venti.
Payne describes a weakness in New Materialism by focusing on the Aetna
and on the account by Friedrich von Schiller (1759-1805) of the modalities
of subjectification. Verde suggests that Theophrastus is one of the most
significant sources of the pseudo-Vergilian Aetna.

Pagliara reviews numerous works of literature and art from the sixteenth to the nineteenth centuries that treat the myth of Aeneas, including that of the Italian philologist Giacomo Leopardi (1798-1837), who saw the Trojans' integration into the native Italian population as an example of humanitas. Coombe suggests that the artistic ekphrases at Aen. 1.446-93, 6.14-36, and 10.495-500 are crucial to the reader's interpretation of Vergil's narrative and that each ekphrasis serves as a synecdoche for the whole. Hamm discusses the woodcuts illustrating the Aeneid by the German humanist and satirist Sebastian Brant (1457-1521). Schier puts forth reasons for believing that, although the painting known as Inferno with Aeneas and Anchises by the Venetian Giorgione (1478-1510) was believed to have been lost, his Il Tramonto is in fact this painting. publication_block: Rónai 8: 19-37. ---. 2021. "Sintaxe mimética nas traduções virgilianas de Odorico Mendes." Rónai 9: 82-99. Ziosi, Antonio. 2017. Didone: La tragedia dell'abbandono; variazioni sul mito. Tascabili Marsilio 349. Venezia. Boyer translates the Georgics into French. Cairolli analyzes the use of the redondilla mayor in seven-line stanzas in the translation of Aen. 1 into Portuguese. Calvert considers a number of English translators of Vergil from the mid-sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, including Abraham Cowley (1618-1667), Edmund Waller (1606-1687), and others. Castro de Castro writes on the translation of Vergil's works, in particular the Georgics, by the Spanish poet Manuel Machado Ruiz (1874-1947) and its influence on the translations and original writings of the Argentine writer Leopoldo Marechal (1900-1970). Cozzolino compares Vergil's Latin text with the Italian translation by the poet Salvatore Quasimodo (1901-1968). Frick offers an edition and discussion of the German translation of the Aeneid (1515) by Thomas Murner (1475-c. 1537). Karlsson looks at the Aeneid translation by the novelist Hélisenne de Crenne (c. 1510-1552). Medina offers a translation of the Eclogues in Catalan. Said writes about the Maltese Dominican priest Albert M. Grech (1883-1942), who translated the first six books of Vergil's Aeneid into Maltese in his unpublished L-Enejjija (1938-1941). His translation was made in what is considered to be a golden era for Maltese, which was recognized by the British colonial government as an official language in 1934. Schwartz considers the translation of Vergil by the English political theorist James Harrington (1611-1677) and its function as an expression for his political thought. Thamos offers a Portuguese translation and commentary. Valenta focuses on the translations by Václav Jan Rosa (1620-1689). Ziosi's compilation includes works by Giovanni Boccaccio (1313-1375), Christopher Marlowe (1564-1593), Pietro Trapassi (1698-1782), Giuseppe Ungaretti (1888-1970), Joseph Brodsky (1940-1996), and others. De Vasconcellos 2020 discusses the translation practice of Antônio José de Lima Leitão (1787-1856), who translated Vergil's works into Portuguese. Boyer translates the Georgics into French. Cairolli analyzes the use of the
redondilla mayor in seven-line stanzas in the translation of Aen. 1 into
Portuguese. Calvert considers a number of English translators of Vergil
from the mid-sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, including Abraham
Cowley (1618-1667), Edmund Waller (1606-1687), and others. Castro
de Castro writes on the translation of Vergil's works, in particular the
Georgics, by the Spanish poet Manuel Machado Ruiz (1874-1947) and its
influence on the translations and original writings of the Argentine writer
Leopoldo Marechal (1900-1970). Cozzolino compares Vergil's Latin text
with the Italian translation by the poet Salvatore Quasimodo (1901-1968).
Frick offers an edition and discussion of the German translation of the
Aeneid (1515) by Thomas Murner (1475-c. 1537). Karlsson looks at the
Aeneid translation by the novelist Hélisenne de Crenne (c. 1510-1552).
Medina offers a translation of the Eclogues in Catalan. Said writes about
the Maltese Dominican priest Albert M. Grech (1883-1942), who translated
the first six books of Vergil's Aeneid into Maltese in his unpublished
L-Enejjija (1938-1941). His translation was made in what is considered
to be a golden era for Maltese, which was recognized by the British
colonial government as an official language in 1934. Schwartz considers
the translation of Vergil by the English political theorist James Harrington
(1611-1677) and its function as an expression for his political thought.
Thamos offers a Portuguese translation and commentary. Valenta focuses
on the translations by Václav Jan Rosa (1620-1689). Ziosi's compilation
includes works by Giovanni Boccaccio (1313-1375), Christopher Marlowe
(1564-1593), Pietro Trapassi (1698-1782), Giuseppe Ungaretti (1888-1970),
Joseph Brodsky (1940-1996), and others. De Vasconcellos 2020 discusses
the translation practice of Antônio José de Lima Leitão (1787-1856), who
translated Vergil's works into Portuguese. His translation of the Aeneid
172 - Shirley Werner
(1819) is compared with the famous "Brazilian Aeneid" (1858) by Manuel
Odorico Mendes (1799-1864).

Fulkerson compares Ciris and Ovid's Scylla episode (Met. 8.1-151) in an
analysis of each poet's distinctive approach to the same story. Kayachev
proposes the readings reicere et indomita uirtute retundere Minon at Ciris
118 (2018a); incerti iactatur flamine uenti at Ciris 478 (2018b); and Sunion
and simul (instead of uenus) at Ciris 471 and 472. At Moretum 20 Kayachev
2020 proposes praebebat in place of seruabat. Lanzarone 2019 offers a
first edition of the notes to Aetna from the school of Julius Pomponius
Laetus (1428-1498) in the manuscript Corsinianus 1839, many of which
are similar or identical to those recorded in the manuscript London,
British Library, Sloane 777, an autograph by Pomponius. Lanzarone 2020
analyzes the notes on Culex 402 and 442 by Pomponius in Oxford, Bodleian
Library, Canonicianus Classicus Latinus 54; in the 1490 printed edition by
Daniele Gaetani; and in the 1544 edition by Oporinus. Olszaniec proposes
reading candida Apollinea florebant tempora lauro at Ciris 121; at Ciris
118 he emends to deicere et indomita Minoa retundere mente; Ciris 484 he
emends to sed talem aeternum squamis uestire puellam. Rosati observes
that the Lydia combines the elegiac voices of Vergil's Gallus (Ecl. 10) and
Ovid's Sappho (Epist. 15). Verde analyzes the pseudo-Vergilian Aetna
from a philosophical-historical perspective to clarify the question of how
widespread the scientific methods of Epicurus and Lucretius were in the
early imperial period.

Arena rejects the prevailing view that numerous statues of Marsyas with a raised arm found in Rome and the provinces function as a symbol of cities' freedom: this view is based on Servius's misunderstanding of the role of the god Liber in the Aeneid. Bovet notes that, according to Servius, Aen. 1.289, the verbs honorare and onerare were frequently confused; inscriptions confirm this confusion. Bureau observes that, in his commentary on Terence, the originality of Donatus's approach to Vergil lies in his attention to the power of Vergil's dramatic discourse and in Vergil's creation of dramatic situations. Esposito analyzes some shared motifs in the biographical traditions of Vergil, Ovid, and Lucan. Estienne compares two different editions of Servius, Aen. 7.188, to establish his list of the pignora imperii Romani. Gioseffi observes that the argumenta to Vergil's work preserved by Servius and Donatus are not real summaries but attempts to address specific rhetorical or textual objectives. Longobardi observes that knowledge of Theocritus's work among late Latin writers is principally due to Servius's commentaries. O'Sullivan 2019 examines the scholia in the ninth-century MS Montpellier H 253 that do not derive from Servius's commentary: these closely resemble scholia in Servius auctus, the Scholia Bernensia (Bern, Burgerbibliothek, MS 167 and 172, and scholia found in Berlin, SB, MS lat. 2o 421; other scholia can be compared to the commentary on Statius's Thebaid attributed to Lactantius Placidus and to the Vatican Mythographers. O'Sullivan 2020 demonstrates ways in which the scholia to Vergil reflect Carolingian imperial ideology, classicism, and historiographical culture. Roesch remarks that at Aen. 9.4 Servius connects the substantive consilium to the verb sedere. Setaioli's analysis of Servius's only reference to Plotinus demonstrates that the daimon Servius describes more closely resembles ideas prevalent in popular superstition than it does Plotinus's original thought. Hejduk explores Jupiter's manifestations in the poetry of Vergil, Horace,
Tibullus, Propertius, and Ovid. Mac Góráin discusses ways in which the
Aeneid reflects the theory of tripartite theology and the identification of
the Penates with the Great Gods of Samothrace, both of which theories
were set out in Varro's Antiquitates rerum divinarum. Soler shows that
Christian and pagan authors of late antiquity did not treat Vergil as a
prophet, but rather subjected his work to allegorical and political readings.

Bencivenga discusses the death of Misenus (Aen. 6.162-235) and the
narrative and Augustan political implications of his burial at the site
of Misenum. Burrichter and Gertz consider the relationship between
the historical visions of Aen. 6.752-853 and 8.626-728 and the pictorial
program of the Forum Augustum. Finn links the "ship of Aeneas"
that Procopius of Caesarea reported seeing near the bank of the Tiber
(Goth. 8.22.5-8) to Augustus's dedication of his Forum in 2 BC and to
the performance of his naumachia. Kerrigan considers the literary,
economic, and political geography of the Georgics and offers a case study
of its British imperial reception c. 1840-1930. Renoux notices that Vergil
accurately describes Vulcan's and the Cyclopes' forges but evokes several
specific forging techniques, transposing reality into the mythical world
and demonstrating Vulcan's superior technical knowledge. After noting
that Vergil connects the underworld river Eridanus with a real river, the
Po, Scarborough observes that Eridanus leads the way from Elysium into
Italy, hinting at the violence that awaits Aeneas in the world where the
river turns dangerous. At the same time, the presence of the river claims
Elysium as an extension of Vergil's native landscape, both geographic
and literary. Tueller observes that, in addition to her other intertextual
resonances, Dido can be viewed through the lens of epigram, especially
sepulchral epigram.

Bernstein finds a dedicatory acronym to Maecenas, in reverse, at Ecl. 6.69
(calamos, en accipe, Musae). Conte 2018 shows that, unlike Lucretius, who
builds syntactic structures through subordinate clauses, Vergil imitates
Homer in his tendency towards parataxis with the use of et, -que, and
sed. Dexter and Chaudhuri note that in the phrase Dardanio Anchisae at
Aen. 1.617 and 9.647 the co-occurrence of hiatus and fifth-foot spondee

the imitation. Gagliardi 2016 discusses the stylistic feature known as

and perhaps originating with Gallus. Meulder explores the double

Audano notes that Suetonius, Tit. 5.3 alludes to Aeneas's encounter with
Anchises at Aen. 6.687-689. Cardigni writes on Fulgentius's interpretation
of the Aeneid as an allegorical journey through the stages of human life.
Florio examines some of the more extreme revisions and reuses of Vergil's
works in later ages, including the works of Petronius, Ausonius, Proba,
and Prudentius. Gilski discusses Mariology in the Homeric and Vergilian
centones, including the Cento Probae. Hosle argues that Ambrose's
allusion at Epist. 73.18 to Georg. 1.149 shows his awareness of the double
significance of Vergil's Dodona both as a metonymy for Jupiter and for its
pseudoetymological association with the verb δίδοναι. Nazzaro observes
that, unlike other Christian authors, who invoke Vergil in terms that are
either laudatory or polemical, Ambrose of Milan (c. 339-c. 397) shows a
balanced attitude toward the Georgics. Perilli notes that Petronius 68.1-2
evokes Aen. 7.117-129. Salanitro analyzes a fifth-century Vergilian cento
which seems to be an ekphrasis of a wall painting. Schirner discusses the
Vergilian allusions at Valerius Flaccus 2.77-432. Sisul 2021b focuses on De
verbi incarnatione.

Arena rejects the prevailing view that numerous statues of Marsyas with a
raised arm found in Rome and the provinces function as a symbol of cities'
freedom: this view is based on Servius's misunderstanding of the role of
the god Liber in the Aeneid. Bovet notes that, according to Servius, Aen.
1.289, the verbs honorare and onerare were frequently confused; inscriptions
confirm this confusion. Bureau observes that, in his commentary on Terence,
the originality of Donatus's approach to Vergil lies in his attention to the
power of Vergil's dramatic discourse and in Vergil's creation of dramatic
situations. Esposito analyzes some shared motifs in the biographical
traditions of Vergil, Ovid, and Lucan. Estienne compares two different
editions of Servius, Aen. 7.188, to establish his list of the pignora imperii
Romani. Gioseffi observes that the argumenta to Vergil's work preserved by
Servius and Donatus are not real summaries but attempts to address specific
rhetorical or textual objectives. Longobardi observes that knowledge of
Theocritus's work among late Latin writers is principally due to Servius's
commentaries. O'Sullivan 2019 examines the scholia in the ninth-century
MS Montpellier H 253 that do not derive from Servius's commentary: these
closely resemble scholia in Servius auctus, the Scholia Bernensia (Bern,
Burgerbibliothek, MS 167 and 172, and scholia found in Berlin, SB, MS
lat. 2o 421; other scholia can be compared to the commentary on Statius's
Thebaid attributed to Lactantius Placidus and to the Vatican Mythographers.
O'Sullivan 2020 demonstrates ways in which the scholia to Vergil reflect
Carolingian imperial ideology, classicism, and historiographical culture.
Pirovano 2022 discusses the rhetorical interpretations of Aen. 8.9-17 and
11.225-295 by Sulpicius Victor, Servius, Servius Auctus, and Emporius.
Roesch remarks that at Aen. 9.4 Servius connects the substantive consilium
to the verb sedere. Setaioli's analysis of Servius's only reference to Plotinus
demonstrates that the daimon Servius describes more closely resembles
ideas prevalent in popular superstition than it does Plotinus's original
thought. Stok 2019 observes that the eighth-century Spangenberg fragment
(Marburg, MS Hessische Staatsarchiv, fr.  319, Pfarrarchiv Spangenberg
[Depositum], Hr Nr. 1), usually considered the oldest manuscript of Servius
Danielis, contains notes not present in the Servius Danielis. It is possible
that the source of the fragment was the lost Vergilian commentary of Aelius
Donatus. Stok 2021 analyzes Ovid's treatment of the story of Achaemenides
(Met. 14.167-222), which rectifies particulars of the Homeric elements of
Vergilian Bibliography 2020-2022 - 195
the Polyphemus episode in Homer that were modified by Vergil at Aen.
3.568-691. It seems probable that Ovid knew the works of the obtrectatores,
in which Vergil was criticized.

of Vergil on a naval scene in an epic poem (1571) written in Castilian by the
Portuguese poet Pedro de Acosta Perestrello. Luke discusses the editorial
change of "bushing" to "blushing" made by eighteenth-century critics
in John Milton's (1608-1674) Paradise Lost in order to evoke Lavinia's
blush in Vergil. Magnien writes about the Vergilian scholarship of Julius
Caesar Scaliger (Giulio Cesare della Scala, 1484-1558). Owens discusses
the nature of ambiguity in Renaissance supplements to the Aeneid,
including those by Pietro Candido Decembrio (1399-1477), Maffeo Vegio
(1407-1458), and Jan van Foreest (1586-1651). Pantani traces Vergilian
influences on the poetry of Giuso de' Conti (c. 1390-1449) and Basinio
da Parma (Basinio Basini, 1425-1457) in their representation of love,
particularly in lovers' use of magic and the persistence of love after death.
Reid discusses Vergilian and Ovidian resonances in the Legend of Chastity
by Edmund Spenser (1552-1599). Silva studies Vergilian influences in the
medical writings of Rodrigo de Castro (c. 1550-1627). Škraban considers
the pastoral poetry of Giovanni Pontano (1426-1503) (in Slovenian, with
English summary). Usher considers Homeric and Vergilian influences on
the poet Pierre de Ronsard (1524-1585).

title: Ruins and Reputations: The Tomb of the Poet in Visual Art
publication_block: In Goldschmidt and Graziosi, 299-316. Gamberale notes that Vergil's representation of Italy's future dominion under the descendants of Aeneas stems from an innovative reading of Il. 20.302-308, an aspect that is captured in the poetry of Giorgio Caproni (1896-1981) and the sculpture of Francesco Baratta (d. c. 1730), Ugo Attardi (1923-2006), and Sandro Chia (1946-). Humfrey writes on the "Aeneas" frieze painted by Dosso Dossi (Giovanni di Nicolò Luteri, 1489-1542). Quaranta interprets the mythological frescoes in Triclinium 8 and Room 34 of the House of Sirico in Pompeii, which illustrate at least two episodes from the Aeneid: the wounded Aeneas's rescue by Venus and his wedding with Dido. Rotiroti reflects on the monument honoring Vergil that was dedicated in Mantua in 1927. The sculptures by Giuseppe Menozzi (1895- 1966) embody a fascist interpretation of Vergil's work. Smiles's focus is on the painting "Virgil's Tomb by Moonlight, with Silius Italicus Declaiming" (1779) by Joseph Wright (1734-97) and on the sketches made near Vergil's tomb by J(oseph) M(allord) W(illiam) Turner (1775-1851). Duke University

Curbet focuses on Vergil as he is depicted in the illustrations of Dante's Commedia by William Blake (1757-1827). Frick considers the work of the German humanist Sebastian Brant (1457/1458-1521) and the Alsatian poet Thomas Murner (1475-ca. 1537). A drawing of Dante and Vergil by Bartolomeo Passerotti (1529-1592) in the Louvre, Département des arts graphiques, inv. 8471, is the subject of Ghirardi's study. Karas considers the painting The Death of Camilla by the Black American painter Bob Thompson (1937-66) and the influence of a drawing by the French painter Nicolas Poussin (1594- 1665). Thompson represents Camilla "as collateral damage of the sort of nation building that necessitates interracial conflict." Long contemplates the significance of the illustrations of the Eclogues on a ninth-century flabellum (liturgical fan). De Carlos Villamarín offers an edition of a medieval allegorical commentary
on the Excidium Troiae, itself a commentary on the Aeneid, together with
a translation into Italian and notes. Haynes considers how ancient and
9HUJLOLDQ%LEOLRJUDSK\ۙۙ
medieval commentaries on the Aeneid by Servius, Fulgentius, Bernard
Silvestris, and others give us insight into four twelfth-century Latin epics:
the Ylias by Joseph of Exeter, the Alexandreis by Walter of Châtillon, the
Anticlaudianus by Alan of Lille, and the Architrenius by John of Hauville.
Kragl (2022) challenges the analysis put forward by Jan-Dirk Müller,
Episches Erzählen: Erzählformen früher volkssprachiger Schriftlichkeit (2017)
of medieval epic narration as a vernacular mode that is distinct from the
Vergilian tradition. The Imtheachta Áeniása discussed by LeBlanc is an Irish
adaptation of the Aeneid of uncertain (eleventh through fourteenth century)
medieval date. Mora presents a Latin edition of the Aeneid commentary by
Bernardus Silvestris (1085-1160), together with a translation and notes in
French. Simpson's analysis includes discussion of the writings of Geoffrey of
Monmouth (ca. 1095-1155).

Catapano investigates the sixteenth-century reception of the "Ille ego
qui quondam …" incipit of the Aeneid. Cesaro provides a first published
edition of La reina, con grave fiamma incesa, a vernacular dialogue in terza
rima between Dido and Anna that is preserved in the late fourteenth-
century manuscript Firenze, Biblioteca Nazionale, MS II.IV.248. Del Amo
Lozano analyzes Vergilian quotations in the work of the poet and satirist
Francisco de Quevedo (1580-1645). Delcorno discusses the Lenten cycle
in the fourteenth- or fifteenth-century manuscript Assisi, Biblioteca del
Sacro Convento di S. Francesco, MS 557, focusing on its use of Vergil's
description of Aeneas's descent into the underworld. Dugaz discusses the
French translation of the Aeneid by Octavien de Saint-Gelais (1468-1502).
Goethals examines the explicit parody of Aen. 1-4 by the Italian satirist
Pietro Aretino (1492-1556) in his account of the catastrophic sack of Rome
made by Charles V in 1527. McOmish considers the Vergilian and Lucretian
influences on Neo-Latin didactic poetry written in Scotland by the doctor
David Kinloch (1560-1617), the educational and religious reformer Andrew
Melville (1545-1622), and the academic and advocate Adam King (ca.
1560-1620). Pereira Couto discusses the pervasive Vergilian reminiscences
9HUJLOLDQ%LEOLRJUDSK\ۙۙ
found throughout the work of the sixteenth-century Portuguese poet and
humanist Ignatius de Morais (Inácio do Nascimento de Morais Cardoso).
Vidén writes, in Swedish, on the translation by Hélisenne de Crenne
(probably a pseudonym for Marguerite Briet, ca. 1510-1552) of the first
four books of the Aeneid.

Calvert analyzes the Vergilian intertexts of Coopers Hill, by the English
poet John Denham (1614/1615-1669), a poem whose second edition in
1640 draws on the Vergilian technique of retrospective prophecy to make
its status as a commentary on the trial and exectuion of Charles, Earl of
Stratford, more explicit. Kaptur discusses the covert allusions made by John
Dryden (1631-1700) in his translations of Vergil to political circumstances
following the dethronement of King James II. Lerer situates the Latin poem
Ad Patrem by John Milton (1608-74) within the context of the early modern
reception of Vergil. Scott discusses the use of the Aeneid as a classical
colonial predecessor by William Strachey (1572-1621) in his account of the
founding of the Jamestown colony in 1610.

Toscano discusses the Revolutionary War poems "To His Excellency
General Washington" (1775), "On the Capture of General Lee" (1776), and
"On the Death of General Wooster" (1778) written by the American poet
Phillis Wheatley, who endured the Middle Passage as a young girl and was
given a classical education while remaining a slave. Wheatley "obfuscates
the Lucanic heart of her piece within a Vergilian body," a strategy that
allows her to "decry a movement committed both to liberty and to slavery's
perpetuation." Vazquez examines Vergilian influences on the poetry of José
Basílio da Gama (1740-95) and Inácio José de Alvarenga Peixoto (1744-93).

4 and in Justin's Epitome 18 on the novel Elissa; or, The Doom of Zimbabwe
(1900) by Henry Rider Haggard (1856-1925). Colombo focuses on the
incomplete and little-known epic poem La Gerusalemme distrutta by Cesare
Arici (1782-1836), which attempts to imitate the Aeneid both thematically
and stylistically. Schmitz discusses the historical novel Salammbô (1862) by
Gustave Flaubert (1821-80), which is set in Carthage.

Falconer, Gordon, Hickey, and O'Brien all discuss aspects of Vergil's influence
on the Irish poet Seamus Heaney (1939-2013). Fletcher casts a critical gaze
on Eneas Africanus (1919), a novel about the loyal slave Eneas's journey
back to the plantation written by the American Harry Stillwell Edwards
(1855-1938). After sketching the earlier twentieth-century background,
Garía Jurado examines documents that reveal how Vergil was interpreted,
recreated, and translated between 1976 and 1992 in Spain. Mac Góráin
discusses the cultural and political reception of Vergil by the nationalist
scholar, cleric, and Irish-language expert Patrick Dinneen (1860-1934).
Pellicer considers Vergilian influences on works by W. H. Auden (1907-
1973), William Wordsworth (1770-1850), Tom Stoppard (b. 1937), and
Seamus Heaney. Pinheiro Hasegawa and Pereira Furtado reconsider the
significance within Brazilian nationalism of Vergil's words Libertas quae
sera tamen (Ecl. 1.27), used by the unsuccessful 1789 separatist movement
known as the Inconfidência Mineira as a motto for their proposed flag. This
symbol was finally made official in 1963. Ruiz de Vergara Olmos provides
an overview of twentieth-century interpretations of the Aeneid.

Bingham offers a four-page musical score for unaccompanied bass-baritone
voice, which was commissioned by the Royal Academy of Music as part of
the 200 Pieces project. The score takes inspiration from Dickens' "Christmas
Carol," Shakespeare's "Hamlet" and "Othello," the poetry of the Scotsman

James Thomson (1834-82), and the works of Vergil. Curbet focuses on
Vergil as he is depicted in the illustrations of Dante's Commedia by William
Blake (1757-1827). Frick considers the work of the German humanist
Sebastian Brant (1457/1458-1521) and the Alsatian poet Thomas Murner
(1475-ca. 1537). A drawing of Dante and Vergil by Bartolomeo Passerotti
(1529-1592) in the Louvre, Département des arts graphiques, inv. 8471,
is the subject of Ghirardi's study. Karas considers the painting The Death
of Camilla by the Black American painter Bob Thompson (1937-66) and
the influence of a drawing by the French painter Nicolas Poussin (1594-
1665). Thompson represents Camilla "as collateral damage of the sort of
nation building that necessitates interracial conflict." Long contemplates the
significance of the illustrations of the Eclogues on a ninth-century flabellum
(liturgical fan). Zolli discusses the bronze Horse Head by the Italian sculptor
Donatello (ca. 1386-1466) preserved at the Palazzo Carafa in Naples and its
legendary attribution to Vergil.

title: Out with the Old, In with the Young? Youth and Old Age in Vergil's Eclogues and Georgics
publication_block: NECJ 52: 31-48. Tortorella, Claudia. 2024. "The Two Female duces in the Aeneid: The [sic] Comparing the Figures of Camilla and Dido through an Analysis of the Common Adjectives Used to Describe Them." SPhV 26: 61-76. Van Geel, Lien. 2024. "Minor Characters, Major Shifts: The Cases of Pholoe, the Cretan Slave (Aen. 5.281-5), and Cretheus, the Bard (Aen. 9.774-7)." NECJ 51: 64-75. Dardenay considers the question of the extent to which the Aeneid might have influenced the iconographic representation of Rome's founding heroes. Gagliardi (2023a) notes that in his duels after the death of Pallas, Aeneas often addresses his dying or dead adversaries with cruel words, in contrast to their appeals to his pietas. Sometimes the dying enemies respond to his sarcasm in a noble way that underscores their emotional superiority. Gagliardi (2023b) notes that the verb immolare appears only three times in Vergil's entire work, all in the Aeneid and always referring to the human sacrifices Aeneas makes in his anger at the death of Pallas in 10.519 and 10.541 and in the murder of Turnus at the end of the poem (12.949). Morgan brings out the further implications of these passages: when Aeneas sacrifices the sons of Ufens at Aen. 10.515-20, Vergil deploys a name expressive of Roman citizenship itself. Ufens evokes the tribus Oufentina, established in 318 BCE as one of the thirty-five voting tribes Vergilian Bibliography 2024-2025 - 105 of Rome. Not only is Aeneas indulging in barbaric ritual practice, but in the process the founder of Rome is apparently attacking the very fabric of Rome. The implication in immolat (Aen. 12.949), Aeneas's word when he says that his slaying of Turnus is a sacrifice, introduces a comparable disquiet. Gallego Cebollada's book studies the various stylistic ways in which Vergil, Horace, and Ovid create portraits of their characters. Giusti argues that Dido in the Aeneid is both the victim of the Romans' racializing of Phoenicians and Carthaginians, and herself an actor of the Phoenicians' racializing of autochthonous people in North Africa. Skoutelas and Smith observe that in Ecl. 6 and G. 4-two poems in which youths bind an old figure with chains to compel a song-Vergil mobilizes the clash of youth and senectitude to comment on the effects of literary tradition on his own work. Van Geel discovers significance in Vergil's seemingly rushed treatment of two minor characters when they are considered in light of Vergil's use of Homeric epic in the intertext-heavy books 5 and 9. These characters function as Vergil's illustrations of Aeneas's movement away from the Homeric world, as the text progresses toward the proto-Roman project that features prominently in the Aeneid's narrative. The Lupercal on the Palatine, which Evander shows Aeneas at Aen. 8.337- 48, was recently identified with a construction found through geophysical inspection in 2007. The documentation, however, leads Boldrighini to conclude that this structure should rather be identified as a first-centuryBCE nymphaeum, whose decorations are similar to those of other underground structures located in Lazio and Campania. An analysis of historical and archaeological data from the sixth to the second century BCE relating to the legend of Aeneas and his landing on coastal Latium leads D'Alessio and De Stefano to point to two defining periods: an older, mythic period, and a period coinciding with the Roman expansionist policy that preceded the renewed interest in Aeneas in the Vergilian age. Giusti takes up again the supposedly outdated debate about the pro- or antiVergilian Bibliography 2024-2025 - 107 Augustan stance of Augustan texts, which Duncan Kennedy's reflections on these points managed to cast in a different light: "'Augustan' and 'AntiAugustan': Reflections on Terms of Reference," in A. Powell, ed., Roman Poetry and Propaganda in the Age of Augustus (1992). Since then, the dichotomy seems useless in a dialectic that must always consider Augustus as a point of reference. Yet scholarship remains divided in its interpretation of Augustus's reign and its effects on poetry. Kennedy's study offers further reflections on the workings of Augustan ideology than previously assumed. A combined rereading of that essay and Hannah Arendt's writings on the ideological language of totalitarian systems (The Origins of Totalinarianism, 1951) opens up new possibilities for interpreting Augustan rule and anti-Augustanism in Augustan texts. Gregori observes that the prophecies, names of peoples, and military actions evoked in the Aeneid are not entirely conventional but are intended to recall historical events chronologically close and referable to Augustan Rome. This argument is confirmable through the evidence of Latin inscriptions and, in particular, through Augustus's Res gestae 25-33. Livorsi picks out a complex web of intertextual references ultimately dependent on Vergil, beginning with the observation that late antique laws are often rhetorically bolstered by expressions of popularized philosophy and theology. In a constitution of Valentinian III against tomb violators (Nouell. Valent. 23), the phrase nec uana fides, which features in the preamble of this law, seems merely an ornamental allusion to Aen. 4.12. The passage in the law might, however, be a reminiscence of Prudentius, Cath. 3.196, who had used the same Vergilian tag to allude to Paul (1 Cor 15:13-17), thus expressing faith in the doctrine of resurrection. The author of the Vergilian cento Versus ad gratiam Domini also redeployed the phrase nec uana fides with reference to the immortality of the soul, and the cento's intertextual relationship with Prudentius may contribute to the evidence about its date. This net of intertextual references (Vergil, Paul, Prudentius) bestows authority on the legal admonition and justifies an appreciation of the constitutions as literature. Maticic finds a connection between Hercules's drainage of landscapes and drying up of bodies and the evocation of hydraulic works and water consumption in Rome in the Augustan period. Mota observes that Vergil's descriptions of the sanctuaries of Apollo at Cumae and on the Palatine, found respectively in Aen. 6 and 8, can be understood as links of temporal intersection between the heroic past and the new configurations of the cult of Apollo as well as the urban interventions carried out by Octavian. Beck's book gives an account of the "simile world" created across Greek and Latin epic poetry over the centuries, including discussion of Homer, Apollonius Rhodius, Vergil, and Ovid. García Domingo discusses Vergil's linguistic variations and innovations of personal names based on the forms Thy(m)b- and Θύ(μ)βρ-. Kennedy discusses the function of metalepsis in the work of Plato, Vergil, and other ancient texts. Silva discovers a telestich in Aen. 8.246-49 that presents the Greek word σῆμα ("portent, wonder, tomb"). The word is significant from its context (the battle between Hercules and Cacus), with the insistence in the Latin poetic tradition on the imagery of light and revelation and the use of the phrase mirabile dictu (8.252), which appears in the same episode. Holzberg observes that Vergil, Horace, Tibullus, and Propertius display conspicuous restraint when talking about or even alluding to Octavian in their poetry written in the thirties and twenties of the first century BCE. Octavian seems to be featured in early Augustan poetry as deus absconditus and only later, in poems composed after the Aeneid, expressly revered as Σωτήρ. Evidence that supports this is offered from texts that predate the Aeneid, including Vergil, Ecl. 4 and 8; Horace, Serm. 1.5; Tibullus 1.7, and others. O'Rourke considers Propertius's final collection of elegies as the earliest concerted response to the poetic career of Vergil in its totality. The analysis reveals an elegiac Vergil as much as it does an epicizing Propertius. Putnam focuses on the multivalent appearances of the concept of pietas in Horace and Vergil. Santos discusses passages from the Georgics and from Columella, Res rustica 9 which refer to the illness and death of bees. The Neo-Latin epic Hesperis by Basinio da Parma (1425-1457) combines two main intertexts: Vergil's Aeneid and the Homeric epics. Smets argues that Basinio's paratextual features, and in particular his marginal annotations, highlight on a metapoetical level the difficulties of combining two closely related but conflicting literary models. Berno's analysis of both the content and the form of Seneca's quotations
from Vergil demonstrates that Seneca uses them not only to convey Stoic
ideas but also to provide a narrative. He prefers passages focused on
a few key concepts: virtue and fighting, god and fate, death. These are
main themes both of the Aeneid and of the epistles. The distribution of
the themes throughout the collection aims in the beginning at improving
one's virtue and then proceeds, toward the end, to an acceptance of death.
Boureau observes that in Epist. 127 Augustine adopts the phrase uoti reus
from Aen. 5.237 to consolidate the Christian concept of the vow (for the

rediscovering the republican idea of civic engagement. Celotto notes that
Persius engages with G. 1.145-49 via a "divided allusion," evoking this

of morals and literature, respectively. Erskine points out that Nemesianus's
Eclogues are the last collection of bucolic poems before the sociopolitical
shifts of the fourth century CE. Within his reuse of characters, tropes,
and narrative structures from Vergil and Calpurnius Siculus, however,
resides a consciousness of contemporary issues. The thematic structure of
his first Eclogue can be analyzed with a view to his historical context: the
eclogue finds recourse not in hoping for a new political golden age but
in the consolatory and preservative power of a poetry oriented toward
the past. Hardie points to the fact that Prudentius's Psychomachia is an
allegorical epic as the feature that marks a decisive break with the tradition
of classical epic. Yet this raises the question of the extent to which its
allegorical techniques are, in fact, Vergilian. The question then arises as
to whether reading Psychomachia with the help of the Aeneid can also
provide insights for reading the Aeneid. Against this background, Vergil's
use of allegorical techniques can be identified, which can be provocatively
described as Prudentian. Hudson considers the literary aims of Vergilius
orator an poeta?, the fragmentary dialogue attributed to Florus. Spence
argues that at Conf. 5.8.15 Augustine uses our recognition of the affiliation

(2024a) explains, that the compositional strategy used by Proba reflects
the exegetical method of typology: by reusing particular Vergilian verses
for biblical characters, Proba creates a typological relationship whereby a
Vergilian type both prefigures and is fulfilled by a biblical antitype. Her
article now proposes an extended model of typology, in which the type
not only prefigures the antitype, but also enfigures it, providing the reader
with a conceptual paradigm through which to understand a supernatural
reality. In the baptism scene (380-414), which serves as a case study, Proba
reuses passages that in the Aeneid describe female characters, hinting at

of women may also allude to the changing nature of these monsters.

the traditions of bugonia represented in the traditions of Vergil and Varro.

Abbamonte and Stok demonstrate that Pomponio Leto (1428-1498)
made use of technical works of classical literature (including Strabo
for geography, Theophrastus's works on plants, Columella, Varro, and
others), to inaugurate a new way of interpreting the Georgics. Aurelio
Brandolini, who was probably in contact with Leto during his stay in
Rome (ca. 1480-1490), wrote an unpublished commentary on the Georgics,
preserved in the Bibliotheca Apostolica Vaticana, MS Vat. lat. 2740,
which presents many aspects of the new trend in Georgics interpretation
inaugurated by Leto. Filieri examines Vergil's farewell to Dante in the
Divine Comedy. Through a study of forty-one translations of Purgatory
22.40-41 published between 1800 and 2020, Funari outlines the evolution
of the meaning of the sacred (sacré) in French language and culture by
examining the reactions of the translators to the Vergilian intertext at
Aen. 3.56-57. The oscillation between choices emphasizing the Vergil
hypotext or the Dante hypotext allows us to formulate the hypothesis
of a slow but progressive retheologization of the Commedia in French
literature. Gervais discusses the "supplements" to the Aeneid composed
in Latin by the Italian poet Maffeo Vegio (1407-1458) and the Italian
humanist Pier Candido Decembrio (1399-1477). Gros Lladós discusses
Vergilian influences on the anonymous fifteenth-century Catalan prose
romance Curial e Güelfa and the concept of otium that Italian humanists
developed from the rediscovery of classical texts. Gwynne analyzes an
account, in Latin prose and verse, of a necromantic ritual performed

often symbolized in female figures. Pugh discusses two eclogues by Dante

were written as a recusatio in response to a verse epistle sent to him by
the humanist scholar Giovanni del Virgilio (d. 1319), in which Giovanni
pressed Dante to compose a martial epic in Latin celebrating contemporary
Italian military victories, which would prove him a worthy successor
to the author of the Aeneid. The Neo-Latin epic Hesperis by Basinio da
Parma (1425-1457) combines two main intertexts: Vergil's Aeneid and
the Homeric epics. Smets argues that Basinio's paratextual features, and
in particular his marginal annotations, highlight on a metapoetical level
the difficulties of combining two closely related but conflicting literary
models.

A travesty, Hardie notes, is the clothing of the lofty matter of epic in
low manners and low language; while mock heroic is the application of
the lofty generic features of epic to undignified or trivial subject matter.
What the two genres share is an incongruity between form and matter.
The Europe-wide fashion for travesties of the Aeneid in the seventeenth
century and the vogue in France and England for mock-heroic poetry
in the late seventeenth and first half of the eighteenth centuries provide

observes that the Aeneid, perhaps more than any other text, has had a
profound impact on Western literature and society. Yet, even though
Homeric receptions of the Trojan War continue to multiply, there have
been remarkably few receptions of the Aeneid in modern popular culture.
